title.raw: 卷七十三 韋賢傳 Volume 73: Wei Xian

sentences.zh: 韋賢字長孺。

translations.literal: Wei Xian, courtesy name Zhangru.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xian’s courtesy name was Zhangru.

sentences.zh: 魯國鄒人也。

translations.literal: A man of Zou in the state of Lu.
translations.idiomatic: He was a native of Zou in Lu.

sentences.zh: 其先韋孟，家本彭城，為楚元王傅，傅子夷王及孫王戊。

translations.literal: His ancestor was Wei Meng; the family’s home had originally been at Pengcheng, and he served as tutor to Prince Yuan of Chu, tutoring Prince Yuan’s son King Yi and grandson King Wu.
translations.idiomatic: His forebear Wei Meng came from a family rooted at Pengcheng; he was tutor to Prince Yuan of Chu and later tutored Yuan’s son King Yi and his grandson King Wu.

sentences.zh: 戊荒淫不遵道，孟作詩風諫。

translations.literal: Wu was dissolute and did not follow the Way; Meng composed a poem to admonish him in the manner of the Airs.
translations.idiomatic: When King Wu gave himself to debauchery and ignored proper conduct, Wei Meng wrote a poem in the classical admonitory style to correct him.

sentences.zh: 後遂去位，徒家於鄒，又作一篇。

translations.literal: Afterward he then left his post, moved his household to Zou, and composed another piece.
translations.idiomatic: He eventually resigned office, relocated his family to Zou, and wrote a second poem there.

sentences.zh: 其諫詩曰：

translations.literal: His remonstrance poem says:
translations.idiomatic: The admonitory poem runs as follows:

sentences.zh: 肅肅我祖，國自豕韋，黼衣硃紱，四牡龍旂。

translations.literal: Solemn was our ancestor; his state began from Shiwei; embroidered robe and cinnabar sash; four stallions and dragon banners.
translations.idiomatic: Our ancestor rose in solemn dignity from the state of Shiwei, robed in emblazoned silk and cinnabar cords, with four horses and dragon-decked banners at his command.

sentences.zh: 彤弓斯征，撫寧遐荒，總齊群邦，以翼大商，迭披大彭，勳績惟光。

translations.literal: With the red bow he went on campaign, pacifying the distant wastes, gathering and ordering the host of states, thereby aiding Great Shang; in turn he struck Great Peng; his merit and deeds alone were glorious.
translations.idiomatic: Bearing the crimson bow he marched to still the far frontier, rallied the many domains, and buttressed Great Shang; he overcame Great Peng in turn, and his achievements shone bright.

sentences.zh: 至於有周，歷世會同。

translations.literal: As for the Zhou that was, age after age they met in assembly.
translations.idiomatic: Under the Zhou our line for generations joined the royal assemblies at court.

sentences.zh: 王赧聽譖，實絕我邦。

translations.literal: King Nan listened to slander; truly he cut off our state.
translations.idiomatic: King Nan of Zhou believed malicious talk and in truth extinguished our domain.

sentences.zh: 我邦既絕，厥政斯逸，賞罰之行，非由王室。

translations.literal: Our state having been cut off, its government then slipped away; the carrying out of rewards and punishments was not from the royal house.
translations.idiomatic: Once our state was gone, its authority scattered, and reward and punishment no longer issued from the Zhou kings.

sentences.zh: 庶尹群後，靡扶靡衛，五服崩離，宗周以隊。

translations.literal: The many directors and host of lords—there was none to support, none to guard; the five domains collapsed and scattered, and ancestral Zhou thereby fell.
translations.idiomatic: High ministers and regional lords offered neither help nor shelter; the five zones broke apart, and the house of Zhou came crashing down.

sentences.zh: 我祖斯微，遷於彭城，在予小子，勤誒厥生，厄此嫚秦，耒耜以耕。

translations.literal: Our ancestor then declined, removed to Pengcheng; as for me the little one, I toil at my life, oppressed by this arrogant Qin, with plough and hoe to till.
translations.idiomatic: Our line then waned and withdrew to Pengcheng; I, a mere stripling, labor to live under the heel of insolent Qin, breaking soil with plough and mattock.

sentences.zh: 悠悠嫚秦，上天不寧，乃眷南顧，授漢於京。

translations.literal: Vast is arrogant Qin; High Heaven is not tranquil; then turning gaze southward, it invested Han at the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Long did arrogant Qin hold sway while Heaven withheld peace; then Heaven’s glance turned south and entrusted the mandate to Han at the capital.

sentences.zh: 於赫有漢，四方是征，靡適不懷，萬國逌平。

translations.literal: Oh radiant is Han; it campaigns in the four directions; whither it goes there is none not won over; the myriad states thereby are pacified.
translations.idiomatic: Splendid Han marched to the four quarters until none who faced it failed to submit, and the myriad regions knew peace.

sentences.zh: 乃命厥弟，建侯於楚，俾我小臣，惟傅是輔。

translations.literal: Then it ordered his younger brother, establishing him marquis in Chu, causing me the petty minister—only as tutor to assist.
translations.idiomatic: He enfeoffed his younger brother in Chu and made me, a humble servant, tutor to the house—my sole charge was to guide the prince.

sentences.zh: 兢兢元王，恭儉淨一，惠此黎民，納彼輔弼。

translations.literal: Diligent was Prince Yuan, respectful, frugal, pure and unified, showing kindness to this black-haired people, taking in those assistants.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Yuan was scrupulous, courteous and spare, single-minded in virtue; he showed kindness to the common folk and welcomed wise counselors.

sentences.zh: 饗國漸世，垂烈於後，乃及夷王，克奉厥緒。

translations.literal: Enjoying the state through successive generations, he handed down blazing merit to those after; then reaching King Yi, he was able to uphold that thread.
translations.idiomatic: The line held the fief through the generations and left a bright legacy; down to King Yi each heir faithfully continued that inheritance.

sentences.zh: 咨命不永，唯王統祀，左右陪臣，此惟皇士。

translations.literal: Alas, the mandate is not long-lasting; only the king carries on the sacrifices; at left and right the attendant ministers—these are the august gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: Alas, no mandate lasts forever; the king alone maintains the ancestral rites, flanked by ministers who are Heaven’s chosen officers.

sentences.zh: 如何我王，不思守保，不惟履冰，以繼祖考！

translations.literal: How is it with our king—he does not think of guarding and protecting, does not consider treading on ice, thereby to continue the ancestors and fathers!
translations.idiomatic: How can our king neglect his duty to preserve the realm, ignore the peril of walking on thin ice, and fail to carry on what his forebears began?

sentences.zh: 邦事是廢，逸游是娛，犬馬繇繇，是放是驅。

translations.literal: State affairs are cast aside, roaming and play are his delight; dogs and horses in long trains—there is releasing, there is driving.
translations.idiomatic: He abandons the business of state for idle sport, and fills his days with hounds and horses loosed and galloped for his pleasure.

sentences.zh: 務彼鳥獸，忽此稼苗，烝民以匱，我王以愉。

translations.literal: He devotes himself to those birds and beasts, neglects these crops and seedlings; the multitude thereby is impoverished, our king thereby is pleased.
translations.idiomatic: He chases birds and beasts while the grain fields go untended; the people grow destitute even as the king finds his amusement.

sentences.zh: 所弘非德，所親非悛，唯囿是恢，唯諛是信。

translations.literal: What he enlarges is not virtue; whom he draws near is not the reform-minded; only the park is what he expands, only flattery is what he trusts.
translations.idiomatic: He promotes no true virtue and keeps no company with those who would mend his ways; he widens his hunting parks and believes only sycophants.

sentences.zh: 睮□諂夫，咢咢黃髮，如何我王，曾不是察！

translations.literal: The fawning men are [text damaged]; the hoary-haired ones are stern—how is it with our king—he never examines this!
translations.idiomatic: Flatterers crowd his hall while white-haired elders speak plainly—how can our king refuse to see what is before him?

sentences.zh: 既藐下臣，追欲從逸，嫚彼顯祖，輕茲削黜。

translations.literal: Having despised the inferior ministers, pursuing desire to follow dissipation, he insults those illustrious ancestors, treats lightly this stripping and degradation.
translations.idiomatic: He scorns his ministers and gives himself to wanton ease, dishonors his glorious ancestors, and treats the threat of disgrace and loss of title as nothing.

sentences.zh: 嗟嗟我王，漢之睦親，曾不夙夜，以休令聞！

translations.literal: Alas, alas our king—kin in amity with Han—does he never early and late, thereby to beautify his fine reputation!
translations.idiomatic: Alas, our king is a kinsman bound in friendship to the Han house—will he never labor night and day to earn a good name?

sentences.zh: 穆穆天子，臨爾下土，明明群司，執憲靡顧。

translations.literal: Solemn solemn is the Son of Heaven; he faces your lower soil; bright bright are the host of officials; they grasp the statutes and show no partiality.
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven sits in solemn majesty over your lands; his clear-sighted officials uphold the law without favor.

sentences.zh: 正遐由近，殆其怙茲，嗟嗟我王，曷不此思！

translations.literal: Correcting what is far begins from what is near; perilous is relying on this—alas, alas our king—why does he not think on this!
translations.idiomatic: He who would set right the distant must begin at hand; to cling blindly to your course is peril—our king, why will you not reflect on this?

sentences.zh: 非思非鑒，嗣其罔則，彌彌其失，岌岌其國。

translations.literal: Without reflection, without mirror, the heir has no model; more and more are his faults; perilously perilous is his state.
translations.idiomatic: He neither thinks nor takes warning; the successor follows no pattern; error piles on error until the state totters.

sentences.zh: 致冰匪霜，致隊靡嫚，瞻惟我王，昔靡不練。

translations.literal: Ice is brought about not by frost alone; a fall is brought about not without arrogance—look to our king—in the past there was nothing in which he was not drilled.
translations.idiomatic: Thick ice gathers from more than a single frost, and a throne topples only when pride has long gathered—look at our king, who in youth was schooled in every branch of learning.

sentences.zh: 興國救顛，孰違悔過，追思黃髮，秦繆以霸。

translations.literal: Raising a state, saving from toppling—who turns away from repenting of faults, who pursues thought of the hoary-haired—Duke Mu of Qin thereby became hegemon.
translations.idiomatic: States are raised and ruin averted only by those who repent their faults and heed white-haired elders—thus Duke Mu of Qin rose to hegemony.

sentences.zh: 歲月其徂，年其逮耇，於昔君子，庶顯於後。

translations.literal: Years and months go on; the years reach old age; as for the gentlemen of old, perhaps they were illustrious among those after.
translations.idiomatic: The years slip by until one is old; the gentlemen of former times hoped at least to leave a name to posterity.

sentences.zh: 我王如何，曾不斯覺！

translations.literal: How is it with our king—he never awakens to this!
translations.idiomatic: How can our king remain blind to all of this?

sentences.zh: 黃髮不近，胡不時監！

translations.literal: The hoary-haired he does not draw near—why does he not constantly watch!
translations.idiomatic: He keeps no wise elders at his side—why will he not look to himself in the mirror of the past?

sentences.zh: 其在鄒詩曰：

translations.literal: His poem at Zou says:
translations.idiomatic: His poem composed at Zou reads:

sentences.zh: 微微小子，既耇且陋，豈不牽位，穢我王朝。

translations.literal: Humble humble the little one, already old and yet base—how could I not be drawn by office, thereby defiling the royal court of the king.
translations.idiomatic: I am but a frail old man, unworthy of rank—yet office tugs at me and I fear I would stain the king’s court by taking it.

sentences.zh: 王朝肅清。

translations.literal: The royal court is solemn and pure.
translations.idiomatic: The royal court is kept in solemn good order.

sentences.zh: 唯俊之庭，顧瞻余躬，懼穢此征。

translations.literal: It is the courtyard of the talented; I look back at my person; I fear defiling this going forth.
translations.idiomatic: That hall is for the worthy alone; I look at myself and dread that accepting this summons would foul its purity.

sentences.zh: 我之退征，請於天子，天子我恤，矜我發齒。

translations.literal: As for my retiring withdrawal, I requested it of the Son of Heaven; the Son of Heaven pitied me, took compassion on my hair and teeth.
translations.idiomatic: I begged leave to withdraw and lay the matter before the Son of Heaven; he took pity on my gray hair and failing teeth.

sentences.zh: 赫赫天子，明哲且仁，懸車之義，以洎小臣。

translations.literal: Radiant radiant is the Son of Heaven, wise, perspicacious, and humane; the righteousness of suspending the carriage—thereby reaching the petty minister.
translations.idiomatic: The glorious Son of Heaven is wise, clear-sighted, and kind; in the spirit of “hanging up the chariot” for old age he extended that grace even to me, a humble servant.

sentences.zh: 嗟我小子，豈不懷土？

translations.literal: Alas I the little one—how could I not long for the soil?
translations.idiomatic: Alas, though I am but a minor figure, do I not love my native ground?

sentences.zh: 庶我王寤，越遷於魯。

translations.literal: Perhaps my king will awaken, and cross over to remove to Lu.
translations.idiomatic: Would that my king might wake to wisdom and consent to move our house to Lu.

sentences.zh: 既去檷祖，惟懷惟顧，祁祁我徒，戴負盈路。

translations.literal: Having left the ancestor at Ni, only longing, only looking back; in long lines my followers, on backs and heads filling the road.
translations.idiomatic: We left our forebear’s grave at Ni with hearts full of longing and backward glances; my people trailed in a long line, loads borne on shoulder and back till the road was full.

sentences.zh: 爰戾於鄒，剪茅作堂，我徒我環，築室於牆。

translations.literal: Then we arrived at Zou, cut thatch to make a hall; my followers, my encirclers, built chambers against the wall.
translations.idiomatic: We came to rest at Zou, roofed a hall with clipped thatch, and my kinsmen and companions raised dwellings along the lane.

sentences.zh: 我即□逝，心存我舊，夢我瀆上，立於王朝。

translations.literal: I then [text damaged] depart; in my heart I keep my old place; I dream on the ford-bank, standing in the royal court.
translations.idiomatic: Though I go forth, my heart stays with the old home; I dream myself standing once more in the royal hall above the river.

sentences.zh: 其夢如何？

translations.literal: How was the dream?
translations.idiomatic: What manner of dream was it?

sentences.zh: 夢爭王室。

translations.literal: I dreamed of contending for the royal house.
translations.idiomatic: I dreamed I was struggling for place in the king’s household.

sentences.zh: 其爭如何？

translations.literal: How was the contention?
translations.idiomatic: What was the struggle like?

sentences.zh: 夢王我弼。

translations.literal: I dreamed the king made me assistant.
translations.idiomatic: I dreamed the king had appointed me his helper.

sentences.zh: 寤其外邦，歎其喟然，念我祖考，泣涕其漣。

translations.literal: Awaking in that outer state, I sigh with deep moan; I think of my ancestors and fathers; tears flow in ripples.
translations.idiomatic: I woke in a distant land and groaned aloud; I thought of my forebears and wept till the tears ran in streams.

sentences.zh: 微微老夫，咨既遷絕，洋洋仲尼，視我遺烈。

translations.literal: Humble humble the old man—alas, already moved and cut off; vast vast is Zhongni—look upon my remaining blazing deeds.
translations.idiomatic: I am a frail old man, severed from all I knew; great Confucius looks down on the little legacy I still might leave.

sentences.zh: 濟濟鄒魯，禮義唯恭，誦習弦歌，於異他邦。

translations.literal: In throngs Zou and Lu—ritual and righteousness only reverent—reciting, practicing, string-songs—thereby differing from other states.
translations.idiomatic: Zou and Lu teem with scholars where rite and right are honored, where the Classics are chanted to lute and song—unlike any other land.

sentences.zh: 我雖鄙耇，心其好而，我徒侃爾，樂亦在而。

translations.literal: Although I am base and old, my heart indeed delights in it; my followers are joyful—joy is also therein.
translations.idiomatic: Though I am old and meanly born, my heart rejoices in this place; my companions are merry, and I too find my contentment here.

sentences.zh: 孟卒於鄒。

translations.literal: Meng died at Zou.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Meng died in Zou.

sentences.zh: 或曰其子孫好事，述先人之志而作是詩也。

translations.literal: Some say his descendants, fond of such things, narrated the intent of the prior man and composed this poem.
translations.idiomatic: Some hold that later generations, eager to commemorate him, set down what their ancestor had meant to say and framed these poems in his name.

sentences.zh: 賢四子：長子方山為高寢令，早終；

translations.literal: Xian had four sons: the eldest son Fangshan was director of the Gaoling shrine and died early;
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xian had four sons. The eldest, Fangshan, served as keeper of the Gaoling mausoleum and died young;

sentences.zh: 次子弘，至東海太守；

translations.literal: the second son Hong rose to governor of Donghai;
translations.idiomatic: Hong, the second son, rose to be governor of Donghai;

sentences.zh: 次子舜，留魯守墳墓；

translations.literal: the second son Shun remained in Lu guarding the tombs;
translations.idiomatic: Shun, another son, remained in Lu to watch over the ancestral graves;

sentences.zh: 少子玄成，復以明經歷位至丞相。

translations.literal: the youngest son Xuancheng, again by clarity in the Classics passing through offices reached chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: the youngest, Xuancheng, won successive posts through mastery of the Classics until he became chancellor.

sentences.zh: 故鄒魯諺曰：「遺子黃金滿□，不如一經。」

translations.literal: Therefore the proverb of Zou and Lu says: “To leave a son a coffer full of gold is not as good as one classic.”
translations.idiomatic: Hence the Zou–Lu saying: “A cartload of gold left to your son is worth less than a single classic mastered.”

sentences.zh: 玄成字少翁，以父任為郎，常侍騎。

translations.literal: Xuancheng, courtesy name Shaoweng, by his father’s privilege became a gentleman-attendant, regularly attending mounted service.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xuancheng, courtesy name Shaoweng, entered office as a gentleman-attendant through his father’s rank and served in the mounted escort.

sentences.zh: 少好學，修父業，尤謙遜下士。

translations.literal: In youth he loved learning, cultivated his father’s undertakings, and was especially modest and deferential to men below him.
translations.idiomatic: From youth he loved books, continued his father’s scholarly path, and was unusually modest toward those of lower station.

sentences.zh: 出遇知識步行，輒下從者，與載送之，以為常。

translations.literal: When going out, if he met an acquaintance walking, he always dismissed his followers, shared the carriage and saw him off—he took this as constant practice.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever he met someone he knew afoot, he would send his attendants aside, offer a seat in his carriage, and see the man home—so habitual was his courtesy.

sentences.zh: 其接人，貧賤者益加敬，繇是名譽日廣。

translations.literal: In his receiving people, to the poor and base he added still more respect; thereby his fame daily broadened.
translations.idiomatic: With the humble and poor he was doubly respectful, and his reputation spread ever wider.

sentences.zh: 以明經擢為諫大夫，遷大河都尉。

translations.literal: By clarity in the Classics he was promoted remonstrance grandee and transferred to metropolitan commandant of the Great River.
translations.idiomatic: For his mastery of the Classics he was raised to remonstrance grandee, then promoted to commandant of the Great River circuit.

sentences.zh: 初，玄成兄弘為太常丞，職奉宗廟，典諸陵邑，煩劇多罪過。

translations.literal: At first, Xuancheng’s elder brother Hong was assistant to the chamberlain for ceremonials; his duty was to serve the ancestral temples and administer the various mausoleum districts—burdensome and tangled, with many faults.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, Xuancheng’s brother Hong had served as assistant chamberlain for ceremonials, charged with the imperial shrines and the tomb districts—a grinding office in which he racked up many infractions.

sentences.zh: 父賢以弘當為嗣，故敕令自免。

translations.literal: The father Xian, because Hong ought to be heir, therefore ordered him to dismiss himself.
translations.idiomatic: Their father Wei Xian, intending Hong to succeed him, ordered Hong to resign his post.

sentences.zh: 弘懷謙，不去官。

translations.literal: Hong cherished modesty and did not leave office.
translations.idiomatic: Hong, out of modesty, refused to step down.

sentences.zh: 及賢病篤，弘竟坐宗廟事系獄，罪未決。

translations.literal: When Xian’s illness was grave, Hong in the end because of matters of the ancestral temples was bound in prison; the crime was not yet decided.
translations.idiomatic: By the time Wei Xian lay gravely ill, Hong had landed in jail on charges arising from the shrines, and his case was still undecided.

sentences.zh: 室家問賢當為後者，賢恚恨不肯言。

translations.literal: The household asked Xian who ought to be successor; Xian was resentful and angry and would not speak.
translations.idiomatic: The family asked Wei Xian whom he meant to name heir, but he was too bitter to answer.

sentences.zh: 於是賢門下生博士義倩等與宗家計議，共矯賢令，使家丞上書言大行，以大河都尉玄成為後。

translations.literal: Thereupon Xian’s disciples Erudite Yi Qian and others, with the lineage kin, plotted together, jointly forged Xian’s order, caused the household steward to memorialize reporting the grand passing, making metropolitan commandant of the Great River Xuancheng the successor.
translations.idiomatic: Then Yi Qian, an erudite among Wei Xian’s students, conspired with kinsmen to forge their patron’s dying instructions: the household steward was made to memorialize the marquis’s death and nominate Xuancheng, commandant of the Great River, as heir.

sentences.zh: 賢薨，玄成在官聞喪，又言當為嗣，玄成深知其非賢雅意，即陽為病狂，臥便利，妄笑語昏亂。

translations.literal: Xian passed away; Xuancheng at his post heard the mourning; again it was said he ought to be successor; Xuancheng deeply knew this was not Xian’s refined intent; then he feigned mad illness, lay in the privy, wildly laughed and spoke in confusion.
translations.idiomatic: When Wei Xian died, Xuancheng was still in post and learned of the death; word came that he was to inherit the title. Knowing this could not have been his father’s true wish, he feigned madness—rolling in the latrine, cackling and babbling incoherently.

sentences.zh: 徵至長安，既葬，當襲爵，以病狂不應召。

translations.literal: Summoned to Chang’an, after the burial, he ought to have inherited the noble rank; because of mad illness he did not respond to the summons.
translations.idiomatic: He was summoned to the capital; after the funeral he was expected to assume the marquisate, but he pleaded madness and ignored the call.

sentences.zh: 大鴻臚奏狀，章下丞相、御史案驗。

translations.literal: The grand herald memorialized the situation; the document was sent down to the chancellor and imperial clerk for investigation and verification.
translations.idiomatic: The grand herald reported the matter; the memorial went to the chancellor and the censor for investigation.

sentences.zh: 玄成素有名聲，士大夫多疑其欲讓爵辟兄者。

translations.literal: Xuancheng from of old had a reputation; the great officers and literati mostly suspected he wished to yield the noble rank and avoid his elder brother.
translations.idiomatic: Xuancheng was widely respected, and many officials suspected he was shamming illness to yield the title to his elder brother.

sentences.zh: 案事丞相史乃與玄成書曰：「古之辭讓，必有文義可觀，故能垂榮於後。

translations.literal: The clerk of the chancellor who was handling the matter then wrote to Xuancheng saying: “Of old, declining and yielding—there must be pattern and meaning worth viewing, therefore they could hand down glory to those after.”
translations.idiomatic: The investigating clerk under the chancellor wrote to Xuancheng: “In antiquity those who declined honor did so in language worthy of the ages, and so left a shining name to posterity.”

sentences.zh: 今子獨壞容貌，蒙恥辱，為狂癡，光耀暗而不宣。

translations.literal: Now you alone ruin your appearance, cover yourself in shame and disgrace, act mad and idiotic—radiant brightness is dark and not displayed.
translations.idiomatic: You alone have disfigured yourself, heaped shame upon your head, and played the madman—so that the luster you might have shown is hidden and never seen.

sentences.zh: 微哉！

translations.literal: How petty!
translations.idiomatic: How mean a thing to do!

sentences.zh: 子之所托名也。

translations.literal: This is what you rely on for a name.
translations.idiomatic: This is the reputation you choose to stand on.

sentences.zh: 僕素愚陋，過為宰相執事，願少聞風聲。

translations.literal: I am by nature foolish and base; wrongly I am clerk to the chancellor—I wish briefly to hear report of the wind’s sound.
translations.idiomatic: I am a dull man who happens to clerk for the chancellor; I would hear even a whisper of your true intent.

sentences.zh: 不然，恐子傷高而僕為小人也。」

translations.literal: If not so, I fear you will harm what is lofty and I will be a petty man.”
translations.idiomatic: Otherwise I fear you will injure your own nobility and leave me looking the villain.”

sentences.zh: 玄成友人侍郎章亦上疏言：「聖王貴以禮讓為國，宜優養玄成，勿枉其志，使得自安衡門之下。」

translations.literal: Xuancheng’s friend Gentleman Zhang also submitted a memorial saying: “The sage king esteems taking ritual yielding as governing the state; it is fitting generously to nurture Xuancheng, not to twist his intent, causing him to be able to settle himself beneath the crossbar gate.”
translations.idiomatic: Xuancheng’s friend, Palace Gentleman Zhang, also memorialized: “A sage ruler prizes courtesy and yielding in governing; Xuancheng should be treated with indulgence, his wishes not forced aside, so he may live content behind a poor man’s door.”

sentences.zh: 而丞相、御史遂以玄成實不病，劾奏之。

translations.literal: But the chancellor and imperial clerk then, because Xuancheng in truth was not ill, impeached and memorialized him.
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor and the imperial censor nonetheless impeached him, holding that he was not truly ill.

sentences.zh: 有詔勿劾，引拜。

translations.literal: There was an edict not to impeach; he was led in and invested.
translations.idiomatic: An edict forbade prosecution, and he was summoned to receive the investiture.

sentences.zh: 玄成不得已受爵。

translations.literal: Xuancheng had no choice but to accept the noble rank.
translations.idiomatic: Left no honorable exit, Xuancheng accepted the title.

sentences.zh: 宣帝高其節，以玄成為河南太守。

translations.literal: Emperor Xuan prized his integrity, making Xuancheng governor of Henan.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan admired his scruples and appointed him governor of Henan.

sentences.zh: 兄弘太山都尉，遷東海太守。

translations.literal: The elder brother Hong was commandant of Taishan, transferred to governor of Donghai.
translations.idiomatic: His brother Hong served as commandant of Taishan before being promoted to governor of Donghai.

sentences.zh: 數歲，玄成徵為未央衛尉，遷太常。

translations.literal: After several years, Xuancheng was summoned to be commandant of the guards at Weiyang Palace, transferred to chamberlain for ceremonials.
translations.idiomatic: Some years later he was recalled as commandant of the Weiyang Palace guard, then promoted to chamberlain for ceremonials.

sentences.zh: 坐與故平通侯楊惲厚善，惲誅，黨友皆免官。

translations.literal: Because he sat in thick friendship with the former Marquis of Pingtong Yang Yun, when Yun was executed, his faction and friends were all dismissed from office.
translations.idiomatic: He was stripped of office for his close ties to the disgraced Marquis of Pingtong, Yang Yun; when Yun was executed, his associates were dismissed as well.

sentences.zh: 後以列侯侍祀孝惠廟，當晨入廟，天雨淖，不駕駟馬車而騎至廟下。

translations.literal: Afterward, as a full marquis attending sacrifice at Emperor Xiaohui’s temple, when he ought at dawn to enter the temple, Heaven rained mud; he did not drive a four-horse carriage but rode to below the temple.
translations.idiomatic: Later, as a marquis charged with sacrifice at Emperor Xiaohui’s shrine, he arrived at dawn in a driving rain; instead of riding in his four-horse state carriage, he came on horseback to the temple steps.

sentences.zh: 有司劾奏，等輩數人皆削爵為關內侯。

translations.literal: The responsible officials impeached and memorialized; his sort, several men, all had noble rank cut to marquis within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: The authorities impeached him, and he and several others were reduced from full marquis to marquis-within-the-passes.

sentences.zh: 玄成自傷貶黜父爵，歎曰：「吾何面目以奉祭祀！」

translations.literal: Xuancheng himself grieved that he had been degraded and his father’s noble rank stripped, sighing and saying: “With what face shall I perform the sacrifices!”
translations.idiomatic: Bitter that he had lost the marquisate his father had held, he cried, “What face have I left to stand before the altars?”

sentences.zh: 作詩自劾責，曰：

translations.literal: He composed a poem impeaching and blaming himself, saying:
translations.idiomatic: He wrote a poem of self-reproach, which begins:

sentences.zh: 赫矣我祖，侯於豕韋，賜命建伯，有殷以綏。

translations.literal: Radiant was our ancestor, ennobled at Shiwei; granted mandate, established as elder—thereby Shang was pacified.
translations.idiomatic: Glorious was our forebear, enfeoffed at Shiwei, charged as a founding lord and charged with bringing peace to Yin.

sentences.zh: 厥績既昭，車服有常，朝宗商邑，四牡翔翔，德之令顯，慶流於裔，宗周至漢，群後歷世。

translations.literal: His deeds already bright, carriage and robes had constancy; he paid court at the Shang capital; four stallions soared; virtue’s fine display, blessing flowed to descendants; from ancestral Zhou reaching Han, the host of lords passed through generations.
translations.idiomatic: His merit shone clear; his chariots and robes matched his rank; four horses bore him to homage at the Shang capital; his virtue was plain for all to see, and blessing passed down the line from Zhou through Han, generation upon generation of lords.

sentences.zh: 肅肅楚傅，輔翼元、夷，厥駟有庸，惟慎惟祗。

translations.literal: Solemn solemn the Chu tutor, assisting and winging Yuan and Yi; his four horses had service; only careful, only reverent.
translations.idiomatic: Solemn stood the tutor of Chu who steadied Princes Yuan and Yi; his team served the realm in harness, ever cautious, ever reverent.

sentences.zh: 嗣王孔佚，越遷於鄒，五世壙僚，至我節侯。

translations.literal: The succeeding king was greatly remiss; crossing over, they removed to Zou; for five generations tomb attendants, reaching our Marquis Jie.
translations.idiomatic: Later kings failed in duty until the line moved to Zou; for five generations they kept vigil at the royal tombs, down to my father, Marquis Jie.

sentences.zh: 惟我節侯，顯德遐聞，左右昭、宣，五呂以訓。

translations.literal: As for our Marquis Jie, bright virtue heard from afar; he attended left and right Zhao and Xuan; by the five rules he instructed.
translations.idiomatic: My father, Marquis Jie, was known far and wide for virtue; he stood at the side of Emperors Zhao and Xuan and by his example schooled the court in right conduct.

sentences.zh: 既耇致位，惟懿惟奐，厥賜祁祁，百金洎館。

translations.literal: Already old, yielding position—only fine, only splendid; his gifts were many; hundred gold reaching to lodgings.
translations.idiomatic: In old age he resigned his post, splendid in dignity; imperial gifts came in streams—gold by the hundred and a mansion besides.

sentences.zh: 國彼扶陽，在京之東，惟帝是留，政謀是從。

translations.literal: He was ennobled there at Fuyang, east of the capital; only the emperor was what he stayed for; policy and planning was what he followed.
translations.idiomatic: He was enfeoffed at Fuyang east of the capital; the emperor kept him at court and followed his counsel in affairs of state.

sentences.zh: 繹繹六轡，是列是理，威儀濟濟，朝享天子。

translations.literal: Continuous, continuous the six reins—thereby arrayed, thereby ordered; awe-inspiring deportment in throngs; he attends court and feasts the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: The six reins run smooth as he sets his team in order; in full dignity he enters court to attend sacrifice before the Son of Heaven.

sentences.zh: 天子穆穆，是宗是師，四方遐爾，觀國之輝。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven is solemn; he is the clan ancestor, he is the model; the four quarters, far and near, gaze on the radiance of the state.
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven sits in majesty, honored as forebear and teacher, while from every quarter of the realm men behold the splendor of his rule.

sentences.zh: 茅土之繼，在我俊兄，惟我俊兄，是讓是形。

translations.literal: The succession of thatch and clod lies with my worthy elder brother; as for my worthy elder brother—he yields, he shows the model.
translations.idiomatic: The noble fief ought by right to pass to my worthy elder brother; he would yield the title, yet his conduct itself sets the pattern all should follow.

sentences.zh: 於休厥德，於赫有聲，致我小子，越留於京。

translations.literal: Oh how fine his virtue, oh how radiant his fame; he left me the little one, crossing to remain in the capital.
translations.idiomatic: His virtue was perfect and his name resounded, while I, the lesser son, was kept behind in the capital.

sentences.zh: 惟我小子，不肅會同，惰彼車服，黜此附庸。

translations.literal: As for me the little one, not reverent at joint assemblies; lazy with those carriage and robes—degraded this vassal.
translations.idiomatic: I, a mere stripling, failed in reverence at the great assemblies, grew slack in carriage and dress, and was stripped of this servant marquis’s standing.

sentences.zh: 赫赫顯爵，自我隊之；

translations.literal: The glorious, shining noble rank—by myself I caused it to fall;
translations.idiomatic: That glorious, exalted title—I myself cast it down;

sentences.zh: 微微附庸，自我招之。

translations.literal: Humble humble the vassal rank—by myself I summoned it.
translations.idiomatic: this mean vassal’s station—I brought it on myself.

sentences.zh: 誰能忍愧，寄之我顏；

translations.literal: Who can bear shame, lodge it in my face;
translations.idiomatic: Who could bear such shame branded on his face;

sentences.zh: 誰將遐征，從之夷蠻。

translations.literal: Who will go far on campaign, following to the Yi and Man.
translations.idiomatic: or willingly march to the ends of the earth in barbarian exile?

sentences.zh: 於赫三事，匪俊匪作，於蔑小子，終焉其度。

translations.literal: Oh glorious the three high offices—not because of worth, not because of making; oh scorned the little one—in the end such is his lot.
translations.idiomatic: August are the three high offices, not won by merit or founding deed alone; I am the scorned stripling whose measure is fixed at last.

sentences.zh: 誰謂華高，企其齊而；

translations.literal: Who says Mount Hua is high? On tiptoe one stands level with its summit;
translations.idiomatic: Who says Mount Hua is too high? On tiptoe you can stand level with its peak;

sentences.zh: 誰謂德難，厲其庶而。

translations.literal: Who says virtue is hard? Exhort the multitude and thereby.
translations.idiomatic: who says virtue is beyond reach? Exhort the people, and they will rise to it.

sentences.zh: 嗟我小子，於貳其尤，隊彼令聲，申此擇辭。

translations.literal: Alas I the little one—thereby doubly at fault; I made that fair name fall, extended these chosen phrases.
translations.idiomatic: Alas for me, doubly at fault: I brought a fair name to ruin and set down these chosen words in my defense.

sentences.zh: 四方群後，我監我視，威儀車服，唯肅是履！

translations.literal: The host of lords of the four quarters—I am watched, I am seen; carriage, robes, and deportment—only reverence is what must be trodden!
translations.idiomatic: The regional lords of the four quarters watch and judge me—carriage, robes, and bearing—reverence alone is the path you must walk!

sentences.zh: 初，宣帝寵姬張婕妤男淮陽憲王好政事，通法律，上奇其才，有意欲以為嗣，然用太子起於細微，又早失母，故不忍也。

translations.literal: At first, the son of Emperor Xuan’s favored concubine Lady Zhang, Prince Xian of Huaiyang, loved administrative affairs and was versed in statutes; the emperor marveled at his talent and had intent to make him heir, yet because the heir apparent had risen from low beginnings and had also early lost his mother, therefore he could not bear it.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan’s favorite, Lady Zhang, bore Prince Xian of Huaiyang, who loved government and knew the law; the emperor admired his gifts and considered naming him heir, yet the crown prince had risen from humble origins and had lost his mother young, so the emperor could not bring himself to replace him.

sentences.zh: 久之，上欲感風憲王，輔以禮讓之臣，乃召拜玄成為淮陽中尉。

translations.literal: After a long time, the emperor wished to move and instruct Prince Xian, assisting him with ministers of ritual yielding; thereupon he summoned and appointed Xuancheng as marshal of Huaiyang.
translations.idiomatic: In time the emperor sought to influence Prince Xian through example and surround him with men who valued courtesy and deference, so he summoned Xuancheng and named him marshal of Huaiyang.

sentences.zh: 是時，王未就國，玄成受詔，與太子太傅蕭望之及《五經》諸儒雜論同異於石渠閣，條奏其對。

translations.literal: At this time the king had not yet gone to his state; Xuancheng received edict, with grand tutor to the heir apparent Xiao Wangzhi and the various scholars of the Five Classics, mixedly debating sameness and difference at the Stone Canal Pavilion, itemizing and memorializing their responses.
translations.idiomatic: The prince had not yet taken up his fief when Xuancheng was ordered to join Grand Tutor Xiao Wangzhi and the Five Classics scholars at the Stone Canal Pavilion to debate points of doctrine, then to memorialize their conclusions in orderly articles.

sentences.zh: 及元帝即位，以玄成為少府，遷太子太傅，至御史大夫。

translations.literal: When Emperor Yuan assumed the throne, he made Xuancheng superintendent of the household, transferred him to grand tutor to the heir apparent, reaching to imperial clerk.
translations.idiomatic: After Emperor Yuan ascended the throne, Xuancheng served as superintendent of the household, then as grand tutor to the crown prince, and finally rose to imperial censor.

sentences.zh: 永光中，代於定國為丞相。

translations.literal: In the Yongguang period, he replaced Yu Dingguo as chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: During the Yongguang era he succeeded Yu Dingguo as chancellor.

sentences.zh: 貶黜十年之間，遂繼父相位，封侯故國，榮當世焉。

translations.literal: Within the space of ten years of degradation and dismissal, he then succeeded to his father’s chancellor position, was ennobled marquis in his old state—glory in his generation.
translations.idiomatic: Within a decade of disgrace he had climbed back to his father’s post as chancellor, recovered the family marquisate in his home commandery, and won the highest honor of his age.

sentences.zh: 玄成復作詩，自著復玷缺之艱難，因以戒示子孫，曰：

translations.literal: Xuancheng again composed a poem, himself setting forth the hardship of restoring after flaw and defect, thereby to warn and show his descendants, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Xuancheng composed another poem describing how hard it was to rise again after disgrace, as a warning to his descendants. It reads:

sentences.zh: 於肅君子，既令厥德，儀服此恭，棣棣其則。

translations.literal: Oh solemn the gentleman; already he made his virtue fine; in deportment and robes thus reverent; easy, easy his model.
translations.idiomatic: Solemn were the gentlemen of old who perfected their virtue, whose bearing and dress matched their reverence, and who moved with unruffled dignity as a model to all.

sentences.zh: 咨余小子，既德靡逮，曾是車服，荒嫚以隊。

translations.literal: Alas I the little one—already in virtue I do not reach; I was with those carriage and robes—through dissipation thereby fell.
translations.idiomatic: Alas for me, whose virtue never matched theirs; I had the same carriage and robes yet through sloth and arrogance brought ruin on myself.

sentences.zh: 明明天子，俊德烈烈，不遂我遺，恤我九列。

translations.literal: Bright bright the Son of Heaven; outstanding virtue blazing blazing; he did not follow my casting off; he pitied me among the nine ranks.
translations.idiomatic: The radiant Son of Heaven blazed with surpassing virtue; he did not abandon me to my disgrace but took pity and restored me among the nine ministerial grades.

sentences.zh: 我既茲恤，惟夙惟夜，畏忌是申，供事靡惰。

translations.literal: I having received this pity—only early, only late; fear and scruple thereby extended; serving duties without laziness.
translations.idiomatic: Once shown such mercy, I rose early and retired late, kept fear and caution ever in mind, and shirked no duty in office.

sentences.zh: 天子我監，登我三事，顧我傷隊，爵復我舊。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven watched over me, raised me to the three high offices, looked back on my wounded fall, restored the noble rank to my old standing.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor watched over me, raised me to the three high ministries, saw the hurt of my fall, and gave back the title I had lost.

sentences.zh: 我即此登，望我舊階，先後茲度，漣漣孔懷。

translations.literal: I then ascended here, gazed on my old steps; former and later such measures—flowing flowing greatly I cherish.
translations.idiomatic: Standing again on that height, I looked toward the steps I once climbed; past and present weighed on me, and tears of longing would not cease.

sentences.zh: 司直御事，我熙我盛；

translations.literal: The director of integrity and those who oversee affairs—I am bright, I am flourishing;
translations.idiomatic: The director of integrity and the overseers of affairs rejoice with me in this renewal;

sentences.zh: 群公百僚，我嘉我慶。

translations.literal: The host of dukes and hundred officials—I have blessing, I have congratulation.
translations.idiomatic: the host of nobles and hundred officials share my joy and offer their praise.

sentences.zh: 於異卿士，非同我心，三事惟艱，莫我肯矜。

translations.literal: Oh strange the ministers—not the same as my heart; the three affairs are only hard—none deigns to pity me.
translations.idiomatic: Yet these high ministers are strangers to my heart; the three offices are a crushing burden, and none will pity my plight.

sentences.zh: 赫赫三事，力雖此畢，非我所度，退其罔日。

translations.literal: Glorious glorious the three high offices—though strength thereby is exhausted, it is not what I measured; withdraw from it—there is no day.
translations.idiomatic: The three great posts shine in glory, yet though I spend my strength to the end, they are not what I would have chosen; I cannot find a day to step back.

sentences.zh: 昔我之隊，畏不此居，今我度茲，戚戚其懼。

translations.literal: Formerly when I fell, I feared not dwelling here; now I measure this—sorrowful sorrowful my fear.
translations.idiomatic: When I fell before, I feared I might never hold office again; now that I stand here once more, anxiety and dread fill my breast.

sentences.zh: 嗟我後人，命其靡常，靖享爾位，瞻仰靡荒。

translations.literal: Alas my later men—the mandate is not constant; peacefully enjoy your positions; in looking up and gazing do not be negligent.
translations.idiomatic: Alas, you who come after: fortune is never fixed; hold your rank in quiet reverence and never grow slack in duty toward those above you.

sentences.zh: 慎爾會同，戒爾車服，無惰爾儀，以保爾域。

translations.literal: Be careful at your joint assemblies; warn yourselves with carriage and robes; do not be lazy in your deportment, thereby to preserve your domains.
translations.idiomatic: Be scrupulous at the great assemblies, keep watch over your carriages and dress, let no slackness mar your bearing, and so preserve your fiefs.

sentences.zh: 爾無我視，不慎不整；

translations.literal: Do not take me as your mirror—I was not careful, not orderly;
translations.idiomatic: Do not look to me as an example, for I was heedless and ill-disciplined;

sentences.zh: 我之此復，惟祿之幸。

translations.literal: My this restoration—only by blessing of emolument is it fortunate.
translations.idiomatic: my return to honor rests on fortune and stipend alone.

sentences.zh: 於戲後人，惟肅惟栗。

translations.literal: Oh alas later men—only reverent, only trembling.
translations.idiomatic: O you who follow after—be reverent, be awestruck.

sentences.zh: 無忝顯祖，以蕃漢室！

translations.literal: Do not disgrace the illustrious ancestors, thereby to multiply the house of Han!
translations.idiomatic: Bring no shame on your glorious forebears, and so give strength to the house of Han!

sentences.zh: 玄成為相七年，守正持重不及父賢，而文采過之。

translations.literal: Xuancheng was chancellor seven years; in guarding correctness and holding weighty gravity he did not reach his father Xian, but in literary patterning he surpassed him.
translations.idiomatic: Xuancheng served seven years as chancellor; he was less steadfast and grave than his father Wei Xian, yet his literary polish exceeded the elder’s.

sentences.zh: 建昭三年薨，謚曰共侯。

translations.literal: In the third year of Jianzhao he passed away; his posthumous title was Marquis Gong.
translations.idiomatic: He died in the third year of Jianzhao (36 BCE) and received the posthumous title Marquis Gong (“Reverent”).

sentences.zh: 初，賢以昭帝時徙平陵，玄成別徙杜陵，病且死，因使者自白曰：「不勝父子恩，願乞骸骨，歸葬父墓。」

translations.literal: At first, Xian in Emperor Zhao’s time moved to Pingling; Xuancheng separately moved to Duling; when ill and about to die, through the messenger he himself declared saying: “I cannot bear up under the grace between father and son; I beg to return my bones and be buried at my father’s tomb.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xian had moved his tomb to Pingling under Emperor Zhao, while Xuancheng’s line was settled at Duling. On his deathbed Xuancheng sent word through an envoy: “The bond between father and son weighs on me beyond bearing; I beg leave to surrender my bones and lie beside my father’s grave.

sentences.zh: 上許焉。

translations.literal: The emperor granted it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor granted his request.

sentences.zh: 初，高祖時，令諸侯王都皆立太上皇廟。

translations.literal: At first, in Gaozu’s time, he ordered that in all the capitals of the feudal kings they establish temples to the Grand Supreme Emperor.
translations.idiomatic: Under Gaozu it was ordered that every feudal king’s capital erect a shrine to the Grand Supreme Emperor.

sentences.zh: 至惠帝尊高帝廟為太祖廟，景帝尊孝文廟為太宗廟，行所嘗幸郡國各立太祖、太宗廟。

translations.literal: When it reached Emperor Hui, he honored Gaodi’s temple as the Grand Founder temple; Emperor Jing honored Emperor Xiaowen’s temple as the Grand Exemplar temple; in commanderies and states he had once favored, each established Grand Founder and Grand Exemplar temples.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Hui elevated Gaozu’s shrine to “Grand Founder,” and Emperor Jing named Emperor Wen’s shrine “Grand Exemplar”; in every commandery and kingdom the sovereign had visited, matching shrines were built.

sentences.zh: 至宣帝本始二年，復尊孝武廟為世宗廟，行所巡狩亦立焉。

translations.literal: When it reached Emperor Xuan’s second year of Benshi, again he honored Emperor Xiaowu’s temple as the Shizong temple; where he had toured on inspection hunts, there also he established them.
translations.idiomatic: In the second year of Benshi (72 BCE) Emperor Xuan added Emperor Wu’s shrine as “Shizong,” and similar shrines were raised wherever he had toured on imperial progress.

sentences.zh: 凡祖宗廟在郡國六十八，合百六十七所。

translations.literal: In all, ancestral temples in commanderies and states were sixty-eight; together they made one hundred sixty-seven sites.
translations.idiomatic: In all there were sixty-eight ancestral shrines in the commanderies and kingdoms, making one hundred sixty-seven establishments in the provinces.

sentences.zh: 而京師自高祖下至宣帝，與太上皇、悼皇考各自居陵旁立廟，並為百七十六。

translations.literal: But in the capital from Gaozu down to Emperor Xuan, together with the Grand Supreme Emperor and the Lamented Imperial Father, each separately beside the tomb established temples; together they made one hundred seventy-six.
translations.idiomatic: In the capital, shrines from Gaozu through Emperor Xuan, together with those to the Grand Supreme Emperor and Emperor Xuan’s father the Lamented Imperial Father, each stood beside its tomb, bringing the capital total to one hundred seventy-six.

sentences.zh: 又園中各有寢、便殿，日祭於寢，月祭於廟，時祭於便殿。

translations.literal: Moreover in each park there were respectively a resting hall and a convenient hall; daily sacrifice in the resting hall, monthly sacrifice in the temple, seasonal sacrifice in the convenient hall.
translations.idiomatic: Each mausoleum park also had a resting hall and a side hall: daily offerings at the resting hall, monthly rites at the main shrine, and seasonal sacrifices at the side hall.

sentences.zh: 寢，日四上食；

translations.literal: At the resting hall, daily four times presenting food;
translations.idiomatic: At the resting hall food was presented four times a day;

sentences.zh: 廟，歲二十五祠；

translations.literal: at the temple, per year twenty-five sacrifices;
translations.idiomatic: at the main shrine, twenty-five sacrifices each year;

sentences.zh: 便殿，歲四祠。

translations.literal: at the convenient hall, per year four sacrifices.
translations.idiomatic: at the side hall, four a year.

sentences.zh: 又有一遊衣冠。

translations.literal: Moreover there was one roaming of caps and robes.
translations.idiomatic: There was also the annual procession of the late emperor’s cap and gown.

sentences.zh: 而昭靈後、武哀王、昭哀後、孝文太后、孝昭太后、衛思後、戾太子、戾後各有寢園，與諸帝合，凡三十所。

translations.literal: Moreover Empress Zhaoling, King Wuai, Empress Zhaoai, Empress Dowager Xiaowen, Empress Dowager Xiaozhao, Empress Wei Si, Crown Prince Li, Empress Li each had resting parks; together with the various emperors, in all thirty sites.
translations.idiomatic: Empress Zhaoling, King Wuai, Empress Zhaoai, Empress Dowager Bo (mother of Emperor Wen), Empress Dowager Zhao, Empress Wei Si, Crown Prince Li, and Empress Li each had a mausoleum park; together with the imperial shrines there were thirty such sites in all.

sentences.zh: 一歲祠，上食二萬四千四百五十五，用衛士四萬五千一百二十九人，祝宰樂人萬二千一百四十七人，養犧牲卒不在數中。

translations.literal: In one year’s sacrifices, presenting food was twenty-four thousand four hundred fifty-five times; there were used guards forty-five thousand one hundred twenty-nine men, invocators, butchers, and musicians twelve thousand one hundred forty-seven men; those who raised sacrificial victims and soldiers were not in the count.
translations.idiomatic: In a single year of worship there were 24,455 food offerings, requiring 45,129 guards, plus 12,147 invocators, cooks, and musicians—not counting those who tended the sacrificial animals.

sentences.zh: 至元帝時，貢禹奏言：「古者天子七廟，今孝惠、孝景廟皆親盡，宜毀。

translations.literal: When it reached Emperor Yuan’s time, Gong Yu submitted saying: “In antiquity the Son of Heaven had seven temples; now the temples to Emperor Xiaohui and Emperor Xiaojing have both exhausted closeness of kinship—they ought to be destroyed.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Yuan, Gong Yu memorialized: “The ancient Son of Heaven kept seven shrines; those to Emperors Hui and Jing have passed out of the mourning generations and should be abolished.

sentences.zh: 及郡國廟不應古禮，宜正定。」

translations.literal: Moreover the commandery and state temples do not accord with ancient ritual—they ought to be corrected and fixed.
translations.idiomatic: The shrines in the commanderies and kingdoms also violate classical rite and ought to be set right.

sentences.zh: 天子是其議，未及施行而禹卒。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven approved his proposal; before it could be put into practice Yu died.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor endorsed the plan, but Gong Yu died before it could be carried out.

sentences.zh: 光永四年，乃下詔先議罷郡國廟，曰：「朕聞明王之御世也，遭時為法，因事制宜。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Yongguang, then he issued an edict first to deliberate abolishing the commandery and state temples, saying: “We have heard that when a clear-sighted king governs the age, he meets the times to make law, accords with affairs to fix what is suitable.
translations.idiomatic: In the fourth year of the Yongguang era (40 BCE) an edict called for deliberation on abolishing shrines in the commanderies and kingdoms. It read: "We have heard that wise kings shape law to the times and tailor measures to circumstances."

sentences.zh: 往者天下初定，遠方未賓，因嘗所親以立宗廟，蓋建威銷萌，一民之至權也。

translations.literal: Formerly when the empire was first settled, the distant regions had not yet submitted; because of places he had personally favored he established ancestral temples—this was to establish awe and dissolve sprouting trouble, the utmost expedient for unifying the people.
translations.idiomatic: When the realm was first pacified and the frontiers had not yet submitted, shrines were raised in places the founder had favored—chiefly to awe the unruly and nip rebellion, the surest way to bind the people.

sentences.zh: 今賴天地之靈，宗廟之福，四方同軌，蠻貊貢職，久遵而不定，令疏遠卑賤共承尊祀，殆非皇天祖宗之意，朕甚懼焉。

translations.literal: Now relying on the numen of Heaven and Earth, the blessing of the ancestral temples, the four quarters share the same track, the barbarians present tribute—long we have followed yet not fixed it, causing the distant and base jointly to undertake august sacrifice—this is probably not the intent of August Heaven and the ancestors; We are greatly fearful thereof.
translations.idiomatic: Now, thanks to Heaven and Earth and the blessing of the shrines, the empire moves on one course and barbarians pay tribute, yet we have long left rites unsettled and allowed the remote and humble to share the highest sacrifices—hardly the mind of Heaven or the ancestors, and We are deeply uneasy.

sentences.zh: 傳不雲乎？

translations.literal: Does not the tradition say?
translations.idiomatic: Does not the tradition say?

sentences.zh: 『吾不與祭，如不祭。』

translations.literal: ‘If I do not participate in the sacrifice, it is as if there were no sacrifice.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘When I do not take part in the offering, it is as though no offering were made at all.’

sentences.zh: 其與將軍、列侯、中二千石、二千石、諸大夫、博士、議郎議。」

translations.literal: Let them with the generals, full marquises, those at two thousand piculs at court, two thousand piculs, the various grandees, erudites, and gentleman consultants deliberate.
translations.idiomatic: Let this be discussed with the generals, full marquises, ministers at full and regular two thousand piculs, grandees, erudites, and gentleman consultants.

sentences.zh: 丞相玄成、御史大夫鄭弘、太子太傅嚴彭祖、少府歐陽地餘、諫大夫尹更始等七十人皆曰：「臣聞祭，非自外至者也，繇中出，生於心也。

translations.literal: Chancellor Xuancheng, Imperial Clerk Zheng Hong, Grand Tutor to the Heir Apparent Yan Pengzu, Superintendent of the Household Ouyang Duyu, Remonstrance Grandee Yin Gengshi, and seventy others all said: “We have heard that sacrifice is not what arrives from outside; it comes out from within, is born from the heart.
translations.idiomatic: Chancellor Wei Xuancheng, Imperial Censor Zheng Hong, Grand Tutor Yan Pengzu, Superintendent Ouyang Duyu, Remonstrance Grandee Yin Gengshi, and seventy others said: We understand sacrifice not as something imposed from without but as rising from within, from the heart.

sentences.zh: 故唯聖人為能饗帝，孝子為能饗親。

translations.literal: Therefore only the sage is able to feast High God; only the filial son is able to feast his kin.
translations.idiomatic: Thus only the sage can truly feast High God, and only the filial son can truly feast his parents.

sentences.zh: 立廟京師之居，躬親承事，四海之內各以其職來助祭，尊親之大義，五帝、三王所共，不易之道也。

translations.literal: Establishing temples in the capital where he dwells, personally himself undertaking the service—within the four seas each according to his duty comes to assist sacrifice: the great righteousness of honoring kin, what the Five Thearchs and Three Kings shared together, is a Way not to be changed.
translations.idiomatic: Shrines belong in the capital where the ruler dwells, so he may officiate in person while the realm sends officers to assist—that is the great principle of honoring ancestors, common to the Five Thearchs and Three Kings and not to be altered.

sentences.zh: 《詩》云：『有來雍雍，至止肅肅，相維辟公，天子穆穆。』

translations.literal: The Odes says: ‘They came in harmony, harmony; they arrived in solemnity, solemnity; assisting were the lords; the Son of Heaven was august.’
translations.idiomatic: The Odes says: "They came in harmony; they arrived in awe; the regional lords stood as assistants while the Son of Heaven sat in majesty."

sentences.zh: 《春秋》之義，父不祭於支庶之宅，君不祭於臣僕之家，王不祭於下土諸侯。

translations.literal: The meaning of the Spring and Autumn is: the father does not sacrifice at the dwelling of a collateral son; the lord does not sacrifice in the house of ministers and servants; the king does not sacrifice among the feudal lords of the lower soil.
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn teaches that a father does not offer sacrifice in a cadet son’s house, a ruler not in a minister’s home, nor the king among the regional lords of the provinces.

sentences.zh: 臣等愚以為宗廟在郡國，宜無修，臣請勿復修。」

translations.literal: We your servants foolishly consider that as for ancestral temples in commanderies and states, they ought not to be maintained; we your servants beg that they not again be maintained.
translations.idiomatic: We therefore hold that shrines in the commanderies and kingdoms should not be kept up, and we ask that they be allowed to fall into disuse.

sentences.zh: 奏可。

translations.literal: The memorial was approved.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor approved the memorial.

sentences.zh: 因罷昭靈後、武哀王、昭哀後、衛思後、戾太子、戾後園，皆不奉祠，裁置吏卒守焉。

translations.literal: Thereupon were abolished the parks of Empress Zhaoling, King Wuai, Empress Zhaoai, Empress Wei Si, Crown Prince Li, and Empress Li—all did not receive sacrifice; only clerks and soldiers were set to guard them.
translations.idiomatic: The mausoleum parks of Empress Zhaoling, King Wuai, Empress Zhaoai, Empress Wei Si, Crown Prince Li, and Empress Li were closed to sacrifice and left with only a token guard.

sentences.zh: 罷郡國廟後月餘，復下詔曰：「蓋聞明王制禮，立親廟四，祖宗之廟，萬世不毀，所以明尊祖敬宗，著親親也。

translations.literal: More than a month after abolishing the commandery and state temples, again an edict was issued saying: “We have heard that when a clear-sighted king institutes ritual, he establishes four temples to close kin; the temples to the lineage ancestors are not destroyed for myriad generations—by this he clarifies honoring the ancestor and revering the forebears, makes plain loving kin.
translations.idiomatic: A month after the provincial shrines were closed, another edict ran: “We have heard that wise kings ordain ritual with four shrines to close ancestors and undying shrines to the founding line, thereby showing reverence for forebears and love of kin.

sentences.zh: 朕獲承祖宗之重，惟大禮未備，戰慄恐懼，不敢自顓，其與將軍、列侯、中二千石、二千石、諸大夫、博士議。」

translations.literal: We have received the heavy burden of the ancestors; only the great ritual is not complete—We tremble and fear, dare not decide alone; let them with the generals, full marquises, those at two thousand piculs at court, two thousand piculs, the various grandees, and erudites deliberate.
translations.idiomatic: We have inherited a weighty charge from the ancestors, yet the great rites remain incomplete; We tremble at the responsibility and dare not decide alone. Let the generals, full marquises, ministers at full and regular two thousand piculs, grandees, and erudites deliberate.

sentences.zh: 玄成等四十四人奏議曰：「《禮》，王者始受命，諸侯始封之君，皆為太祖。

translations.literal: Xuancheng and forty-four others submitted deliberation saying: “According to the Rites, when the king first receives the mandate, and the lord who is first enfeoffed among the feudal lords—each is Grand Founder.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xuancheng and forty-four colleagues reported: “The Rites state that the king who first receives the mandate and the first enfeoffed lord of a state are each honored as Grand Founder.

sentences.zh: 以下，五廟而迭毀，毀廟之主臧乎太祖，五年而再殷祭，言一禘祫也。

translations.literal: Below that, five temples and in turn destroy; the lords of destroyed temples are stored with the Grand Founder; within five years again the grand combined sacrifice—this speaks of one di and xia.
translations.idiomatic: Below them stand five shrines, rotated and abolished in turn; tablets from abolished shrines are housed with the Grand Founder, and every five years a grand joint sacrifice—the di and xia rites—unites them.

sentences.zh: 祫祭者，毀廟與未毀廟之主皆合食於太祖，父為昭，子為穆，孫復為昭，古之正禮也。

translations.literal: As for the xia sacrifice: lords of destroyed temples and of not-yet-destroyed temples all join feasting at the Grand Founder; father is zhao, son is mu, grandson again is zhao—this is the correct ritual of antiquity.
translations.idiomatic: At the xia offering, tablets from both abolished and standing shrines feast together at the Grand Founder’s hall in the classical zhao-mu alternation of father, son, and grandson.

sentences.zh: 《祭義》曰：『王者禘其祖自出，以其祖配之，而立四廟。』

translations.literal: The Meaning of Sacrifices says: ‘The king performs di to the ancestor from whom he descends, matching him with that ancestor, and establishes four temples.’
translations.idiomatic: The Meaning of Sacrifices says: The king performs the great di to the ultimate ancestor and matches him in Heaven, while maintaining four shrines to close kin.

sentences.zh: 言始受命而王，祭天以其祖配，而不為立廟，親盡也。

translations.literal: This speaks of first receiving the mandate and becoming king: sacrificing to Heaven he matches his ancestor with it, yet does not for him establish a temple—kinship is exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: This means that the first recipient of the mandate sacrifices to Heaven with his ancestor as correlative but builds no separate shrine because that kinship has passed out of the mourning grades.

sentences.zh: 立親廟四，親親也。

translations.literal: Establishing four temples to close kin—loving kin.
translations.idiomatic: The four shrines to close kin express love of family.

sentences.zh: 親盡而迭毀，親疏之殺，示有終也。

translations.literal: When kinship is exhausted then in turn destroy—the gradation of close and distant kin, showing there is an end.
translations.idiomatic: When mourning ties end, shrines are rotated away in sequence, marking the gradation of near and distant kin and showing that honor too has its limit.

sentences.zh: 周之所以七廟者，以後稷始封，文王、武王受命而王，是以三廟不毀，與親廟四而七。

translations.literal: The reason Zhou had seven temples was because Hou Ji was first enfeoffed, King Wen and King Wu received the mandate and became kings—therefore three temples were not destroyed, together with four kin temples making seven.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou kept seven shrines because Hou Ji was the first enfeoffment and Kings Wen and Wu received the mandate—three perpetual shrines plus four for close kin made seven.

sentences.zh: 非有後稷始封，文、武受命之功者，皆當親盡而毀。

translations.literal: Those who lack the merit of Hou Ji’s first enfeoffment, Wen and Wu’s receiving the mandate—all ought when kinship is exhausted to be destroyed.
translations.idiomatic: Any line without the founding merit of Hou Ji or the mandate won by Wen and Wu should lose its shrine when kinship is exhausted.

sentences.zh: 成王成二聖之業，制禮作樂，功德茂盛，廟猶不世，以行為謚而已。

translations.literal: King Cheng completed the enterprise of the two sages, instituted ritual and made music—merit and virtue luxuriant—yet his temple still was not generation-perpetual; by conduct he had posthumous title only.
translations.idiomatic: King Cheng finished the work of the two sages and set ritual and music; though his merit was great, his shrine was not made perpetual—only his posthumous name by conduct survives.

sentences.zh: 《禮》，廟在大門之內，不敢遠親也。

translations.literal: According to the Rites, the temple is inside the great gate—dare not distance kin.
translations.idiomatic: The Rites place the ancestral shrine inside the main gate so the dead are never far from the living.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為高帝受命定天下，宜為帝者太祖之廟，世世不毀，承後屬盡者宜毀。

translations.literal: We your servants foolishly consider that Gaodi received the mandate and settled the empire—he ought to be the Grand Founder temple for the one who is emperor, generation after generation not destroyed; those who inherit after, when affiliation is exhausted, ought to be destroyed.
translations.idiomatic: We hold that Gaozu, who received the mandate and settled the realm, should stand as the perpetual Grand Founder of Han, while later shrines should be abolished when their lines pass out of the mourning generations.

sentences.zh: 今宗廟異處，昭穆不序，宜入就太祖廟而序昭穆如禮。

translations.literal: Now the ancestral temples are in different places; zhao and mu are not ordered—it is fitting to enter and draw near the Grand Founder temple and order zhao and mu according to ritual.
translations.idiomatic: Today the shrines stand scattered and the zhao-mu order is confused; they should be gathered at the Grand Founder’s temple and arranged according to classical precedence.

sentences.zh: 太上皇、孝惠、孝文、孝景廟皆親盡宜毀，皇考廟親未盡，如故。」

translations.literal: The Grand Supreme Emperor, Xiaohui, Xiaowen, Xiaojing temples—all kinship exhausted, ought to be destroyed; the Imperial Father temple—kinship not yet exhausted—according to precedent.
translations.idiomatic: The shrines to the Grand Supreme Emperor and to Emperors Hui, Wen, and Jing should be abolished as remote, while the shrine to the Imperial Father remains while kinship endures.

sentences.zh: 大司馬車騎將軍許嘉等二十九人以為，孝文皇帝除誹謗，去肉刑，躬節儉，不受獻，罪人不帑，不私其利，出美人，重絕人類，賓賜長老，收恤孤獨，德厚侔天地，利澤施四海，宜為帝者太宗之廟。

translations.literal: Grand marshal and chariot-and-cavalry general Xu Jia and twenty-nine others considered that Emperor Xiaowen abolished laws against slander, removed mutilating punishments, personally was frugal and restrained, did not accept presents, criminals were not punished with confiscation of kin, did not privatize profit for himself, released the fair ladies, heavily cut off human lines, hospitably bestowed on the aged, gathered and pitied the orphaned and alone—virtue thick matching Heaven and Earth, benefit and grace extending to the four seas—he ought to be the Grand Exemplar temple for the one who is emperor.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Marshal Xu Jia and twenty-nine others argued that Emperor Wen had abolished slander laws and corporal mutilation, lived in personal austerity, refused tribute, spared criminals’ families confiscation, took no private profit from the realm, released palace women so they might bear children, honored elders with gifts, and sheltered the orphaned—virtue as deep as Heaven and earth, bounty reaching the four seas—and therefore deserved a perpetual shrine as Grand Exemplar of Han.

sentences.zh: 廷尉忠以為，孝武皇帝改正朔，易服色，攘四夷，宜為世宗之廟。

translations.literal: Commandant of justice Zhong considered that Emperor Xiaowu corrected the first month of the year, changed robe and banner colors, repelled the four barbarians—he ought to be the Shizong temple.
translations.idiomatic: Commandant of Justice Zhong held that Emperor Wu had reformed the calendar, changed court regalia, and driven back the barbarians on every side, and so merited a standing shrine as Shizong.

sentences.zh: 諫大夫尹更始等十八人以為，皇考廟上序於昭穆，非正禮，宜毀。

translations.literal: Remonstrance grandee Yin Gengshi and eighteen others considered that the Imperial Father temple in ordering above in zhao-mu was not correct ritual—it ought to be destroyed.
translations.idiomatic: Remonstrance Grandee Yin Gengshi and eighteen others held that placing the shrine to Emperor Xuan’s father in the zhao-mu sequence violated canonical rite and should be abolished.

sentences.zh: 議者又以為《清廟》之詩言交神之禮無不清靜，今衣冠出遊，有車騎之眾，風雨之氣，非所謂清靜也。

translations.literal: The deliberators also considered that the poem “Clear Temple” speaks of the rite of interfacing with spirits—there is none not clear and still; now caps and robes go roaming abroad, there is the multitude of chariots and horsemen, the air of wind and rain—this is not what is called clear and still.
translations.idiomatic: Some argued that the ode “The Clear Temple” describes communion with the spirits as always pure and still, whereas the procession of imperial vestments abroad, with crowds of chariots and exposure to wind and rain, is anything but stillness.

sentences.zh: 「祭不欲數，數則瀆，瀆則不敬。」

translations.literal: ‘Sacrifice does not desire frequency; if frequent then it is treated lightly; if treated lightly then it is not reverent.’
translations.idiomatic: The classic warns that sacrifice must not be too frequent, for frequency breeds contempt, and contempt is irreverence.

sentences.zh: 宜復古禮，四時祭於廟，諸寢園日月間祀皆可勿復修。

translations.literal: It is fitting to restore ancient ritual: the four seasons sacrifice at the temple; the various resting-parks’ monthly and daily interval sacrifices all may not again be maintained.
translations.idiomatic: They urged a return to the old pattern—seasonal offerings at the main shrines—and an end to the costly monthly and daily rites at the mausoleum resting halls.

sentences.zh: 上亦不改也。

translations.literal: The sovereign also did not change it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor left these practices unchanged.

sentences.zh: 明年，玄成復言：「古者制禮，別尊卑貴賤，國君之母非適不得配食，則薦於寢，身沒而已。

translations.literal: The next year, Xuancheng again spoke: “In antiquity when they instituted ritual, they distinguished noble and base, high and low; if a state lord’s mother was not principal wife she could not receive joint sacrifice, then she was offered in the resting chamber, and when her person passed that was all.
translations.idiomatic: The following year Xuancheng memorialized again: “Ancient ritual distinguished rank high and low: a ruler’s mother who was not principal consort could not share the grand sacrifice and received offerings only in the side hall until her death.

sentences.zh: 陛下躬至孝，承天心，建祖宗，定迭毀，序昭穆，大禮既定，孝文太后、孝昭太后寢祠園宜如禮勿復修。」

translations.literal: Your Majesty personally reaches utmost filiality, receives Heaven’s mind, establishes the ancestors, fixes rotation and destruction, orders zhao and mu; the great ritual already fixed—Empress Dowager Xiaowen’s and Empress Dowager Xiaozhao’s resting shrines and parks ought according to ritual not again be maintained.
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty embodies the highest filial piety, follows Heaven’s intent, has fixed the rotation of shrines and the zhao-mu order; now that the great rites are settled, the resting shrines and parks of Empress Dowagers Wen and Zhao should, as classical usage requires, be allowed to lapse.

sentences.zh: 奏可。

translations.literal: The memorial was approved.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor approved the memorial.

sentences.zh: 又告謝毀廟曰：「往者大臣以為，在昔帝王承祖宗之休典，取象於天地，天序五行，人親五屬，天子奉天，故率其意而尊其制。

translations.literal: Again he announced thanks for destroying temples, saying: “Formerly the great officers considered that in the past emperors and kings received the fine models of the ancestors, took images from Heaven and Earth; Heaven orders the five phases, men are intimate in five kin-groups; the Son of Heaven serves Heaven, therefore he follows its intent and honors its system.
translations.idiomatic: A further proclamation thanked the spirits for the abolition of shrines: “Ministers once held that ancient kings took pattern from Heaven and earth—Heaven’s five phases and mankind’s five degrees of kin—so the Son of Heaven, serving Heaven, modeled his institutions on that order.

sentences.zh: 是以禘嘗之序，靡有過五。

translations.literal: Therefore in the sequence of di and seasonal offerings, there was none exceeding five.
translations.idiomatic: Hence the sequence of the great di and seasonal sacrifices never exceeded five in number.

sentences.zh: 受命之君躬接於天，萬世不墮。

translations.literal: The lord who receives the mandate personally connects with Heaven; for myriad generations he does not fall.
translations.idiomatic: The founder who receives the mandate stands in direct relation to Heaven and must never be removed from the line of sacrifice.

sentences.zh: 繼烈以下，五廟而遷，上陳太祖，間歲而祫，其道應天，故福祿永終。

translations.literal: Below continuing the blazing merit, five temples then are shifted; presenting above to the Grand Founder, in alternate years the xia—their Way responds to Heaven, therefore blessing and emolument end forever.
translations.idiomatic: From the heirs who continue the ancestral flame, five shrines rotate in turn, their tablets rise to the Grand Founder, and the great xia unites them every other year—a pattern that answers Heaven and secures lasting blessing.

sentences.zh: 太上皇非受命而屬盡，義則當遷。

translations.literal: The Grand Supreme Emperor did not receive the mandate, and kin-ties are exhausted—by righteousness then he ought to be relocated.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Supreme Emperor never received the mandate, and mourning kinship has run out; by every rule his shrine should be moved aside.

sentences.zh: 又以為孝莫大於嚴父，故父之所尊子不敢不承，父之所異子不敢同。

translations.literal: They also considered that of filial piety none is greater than revering the father; therefore what the father honors the son dare not fail to uphold; what the father sets apart the son dare not treat the same.
translations.idiomatic: They further argued that the highest filial duty is to honor the father: a son must uphold whatever his father honored and must not claim parity with what his father deliberately set apart.

sentences.zh: 禮，公子不得為母信，為後則於子祭，於孫止，尊祖嚴父之義也。

translations.literal: By ritual, a duke’s son may not for his mother observe the extended mourning; when he becomes heir then sacrifice at the son, at the grandson stops—the meaning of honoring the ancestor and revering the father.
translations.idiomatic: Ritual does not allow a noble heir to prolong mourning for his mother as an ordinary son would; once he succeeds, sacrifice reaches his own son’s generation and ends with the grandson—such is the logic of exalting the lineage and honoring the father.

sentences.zh: 寢日四上食，園廟間祠，皆可亡修。

translations.literal: Resting hall daily four food presentations; park temple interval sacrifices—all may cease to be maintained.
translations.idiomatic: The four daily meals at the resting halls and the periodic rites at the mausoleum shrines may all be discontinued.

sentences.zh: 皇帝思慕悼懼，未敢盡從。

translations.literal: The emperor thought longingly, mourned, and feared, and did not dare fully follow.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor grieved and hesitated, and did not dare adopt every recommendation.

sentences.zh: 惟念高皇帝聖德茂盛，受命溥將，欽若稽古，承順天心，子孫本支，陳錫亡疆。

translations.literal: Only considering Gaodi’s sagely virtue luxuriant, receiving the mandate broadly extending, reverently he accorded with antiquity, received and followed Heaven’s mind; sons and grandsons root and branch, arrayed bestowal without limit.
translations.idiomatic: He reflected that Gaozu’s sacred virtue flourished, that his mandate spread far and wide, that he reverently modeled antiquity and obeyed Heaven’s will, and that his descendants—main line and cadet branches—have received blessings without end.

sentences.zh: 誠以為遷廟合祭，久長之策，高皇帝之意，乃敢不聽？

translations.literal: Truly considering that relocating temples and combining sacrifice is a long-lasting strategy—Gaodi’s intent—how dare one not heed?
translations.idiomatic: If merging the shrines and joint sacrifice is truly the lasting policy Gaozu intended, who would dare refuse?

sentences.zh: 即以令日遷太上、孝惠廟，孝文太后、孝昭太后寢，將以昭祖宗之德，順天人之序，定無窮之業。

translations.literal: Therefore on this appointed day relocate the Grand Supreme and Xiaohui temples, the resting halls of Empress Dowager Xiaowen and Empress Dowager Xiaozhao—thereby to display the ancestors’ virtue, accord with the order of Heaven and man, fix an endless enterprise.
translations.idiomatic: On this chosen day, then, let the shrines to the Grand Supreme Emperor and Emperor Hui be moved, together with the resting halls of Empress Dowagers Wen and Zhao, to make plain the virtue of the ancestors, align human order with Heaven, and secure the dynasty for ages to come.

sentences.zh: 今皇帝未受茲福，乃有不能共職之疾。

translations.literal: Now the emperor has not yet received this blessing; then there is the illness of being unable jointly to perform duty.
translations.idiomatic: Yet the present sovereign has not enjoyed this blessing, for illness has left him unable to discharge his duties.

sentences.zh: 皇帝願復修承祀，臣衡等咸以為禮不得。

translations.literal: The emperor wishes again to repair and continue sacrifice; your servants Heng and others all consider that by ritual it is not permitted.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor wished to restore the suspended rites, but Heng and his colleagues held that ritual would not permit it.

sentences.zh: 如不合高皇帝、孝惠皇帝、孝文皇帝、孝武皇帝、孝昭皇帝、孝宣皇帝、太上皇、孝文太后、孝昭太后之意，罪盡在臣衡等，當受其咎。

translations.literal: If it does not accord with the mind of Gaodi, Emperor Xiaohui, Emperor Xiaowen, Emperor Xiaowu, Emperor Xiaozhao, Emperor Xiaoxuan, the Grand Supreme Emperor, Empress Dowager Xiaowen, Empress Dowager Xiaozhao—the guilt is entirely on your servants Heng and others; they ought to bear the blame.
translations.idiomatic: If this should offend the spirits of Gaozu, Emperors Hui, Wen, Wu, Zhao, and Xuan, the Grand Supreme Emperor, and Empress Dowagers Wen and Zhao, let the full blame fall on Heng and his associates.

sentences.zh: 今皇帝尚未平，詔中朝臣具復毀廟之文。

translations.literal: Now the emperor is not yet recovered; he edicts the inner-court ministers fully to restore the texts on destroying temples.
translations.idiomatic: Because the emperor was still unwell, he ordered the inner court to set out in full the documents justifying the abolition of shrines.

sentences.zh: 臣衡中朝臣咸復以為天子之祀義有所斷，禮有所承，違統背制，不可以奉先祖，皇天不祐，鬼神不饗。

translations.literal: Your servant Heng and the inner-court ministers all again considered that in the Son of Heaven’s sacrifice meaning has what is decided, ritual has what is received; violating the lineage and turning against the system—one cannot thereby serve the prior ancestors; High Heaven will not bless, ghosts and spirits will not feast.
translations.idiomatic: Heng and the inner court reaffirmed that the Son of Heaven’s sacrifices rest on fixed principles and inherited rite; to violate the canonical line and break the regulations is to fail the ancestors, forfeit Heaven’s favor, and leave the spirits unaccepting of the offerings.

sentences.zh: 《六藝》所載皆言不當，無所依緣以作其文。

translations.literal: What the Six Arts record all says is not appropriate; there is nothing to rely on or cling to in order to make those texts.
translations.idiomatic: The Six Classics nowhere authorize a reversal; there is no textual basis on which to draft a reprieve.

sentences.zh: 事如失指，罪乃在臣衡，當深受其殃。

translations.literal: If the affair misses the point, the guilt then is on your servant Heng; he ought deeply to receive its calamity.
translations.idiomatic: If the policy should prove wrong, let Heng bear the full punishment.

sentences.zh: 皇帝宜厚蒙祉福，嘉氣日興，疾病平復，永保宗廟，與天亡極，群生百神，有所歸息。」

translations.literal: The emperor ought thickly to receive blessing and fortune; auspicious qi daily to rise; illness and disease peacefully to recover; forever to protect the ancestral temples; with Heaven without limit; the host of living beings and hundred spirits—to have where to return and rest.
translations.idiomatic: May the emperor be richly blessed, see auspicious omens daily increase, regain full health, guard the shrines forever, share Heaven’s endless span, and give gods and men alike a settled place to rest.

sentences.zh: 諸廟皆同文。

translations.literal: The various temples all had the same text.
translations.idiomatic: The same wording was proclaimed at every shrine.

sentences.zh: 久之，上疾連年，遂盡復諸所罷寢廟園，皆修祀如故，初，上定迭毀禮，獨尊孝文廟為太宗，而孝武廟親未盡，故未毀。

translations.literal: After a long time, the sovereign’s illness continued year after year; then he fully restored all the resting halls, temples, and parks that had been abolished, all repairing sacrifice as before. At first, when the sovereign had fixed the ritual of rotating destruction, he alone honored Emperor Xiaowen’s temple as Grand Exemplar, while Emperor Xiaowu’s temple’s kin was not yet exhausted, therefore it was not destroyed.
translations.idiomatic: Years of illness followed, and the emperor restored every mausoleum shrine and park he had closed, reviving the old rites. Earlier, when he had fixed the rotation of shrines, he had singled out Emperor Wen’s temple as Grand Exemplar and left Emperor Wu’s standing because mourning kinship had not yet lapsed.

sentences.zh: 上於是乃復申明之，曰：「孝宣皇帝尊孝武廟曰世宗，損益之禮，不敢有與焉。

translations.literal: The sovereign thereupon again made clear and declared, saying: “Emperor Xiaoxuan honored Emperor Xiaowu’s temple as Shizong; as for the rites of reduction and increase, We dare not have a part therein.
