translations.idiomatic: He then issued a clarification: “Emperor Xuan named Emperor Wu’s shrine Shizong; We may not alter what he ordained touching the addition or subtraction of rites.

sentences.zh: 他皆如舊制。」

translations.literal: In other respects all is as the old system.
translations.idiomatic: Everything else follows the former regulations.

sentences.zh: 唯郡國廟遂廢雲。

translations.literal: Only the commandery and state temples then were abolished, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: Only the shrines in the commanderies and kingdoms remained abolished.

sentences.zh: 成帝崩，哀帝即位。

translations.literal: Emperor Cheng passed away; Emperor Ai assumed the throne.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Cheng died, Emperor Ai ascended the throne.

sentences.zh: 丞相孔光、大司空何武奏言：「永光五年制書，高皇帝為漢太祖，孝文皇帝為太宗。

translations.literal: Chancellor Kong Guang and grand minister of works He Wu submitted saying: “In the fifth year of Yongguang the edict declared Gaodi as Han’s Grand Founder, Emperor Xiaowen as Grand Exemplar.
translations.idiomatic: Chancellor Kong Guang and Grand Minister of Works He Wu memorialized: “The edict of the fifth year of Yongguang (39 BCE) named Gaozu Grand Founder of Han and Emperor Wen Grand Exemplar.

sentences.zh: 建昭五年制書，孝武皇帝為世宗。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Jianzhao the edict declared Emperor Xiaowu as Shizong.
translations.idiomatic: The fifth year of Jianzhao (34 BCE) proclaimed Emperor Wu Shizong.

sentences.zh: 損益之禮，不敢有與。

translations.literal: As for the rites of reduction and increase, We dare not have a part.
translations.idiomatic: We may not tamper with those additions to the ritual.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為迭毀之次，當以時定，非令所為擅議宗廟之意也。

translations.literal: We your servants foolishly consider that the sequence of rotating destruction ought to be fixed according to the times; it is not the intent of the present edict to permit private deliberation on the ancestral temples.
translations.idiomatic: We hold that the order in which shrines are rotated out should be settled in due course; the present order was never meant to invite arbitrary debate over the imperial temples.

sentences.zh: 臣請與群臣雜議。」

translations.literal: We your servants beg to join the host of ministers in mixed deliberation.
translations.idiomatic: We ask leave to deliberate jointly with the full court.

sentences.zh: 奏可。

translations.literal: The memorial was approved.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor approved.

sentences.zh: 於是，光祿勳彭宣、詹事滿昌、博士左咸等五十三人皆以為繼祖宗以下，五廟而迭毀，後雖有賢君，猶不得與祖宗並列。

translations.literal: Thereupon, superintendent of the imperial household Peng Xuan, supervisor of the household Man Chang, erudite Zuo Xian, and fifty-three others all considered that continuing the ancestors below, five temples then in turn destroyed—even if afterward there is a worthy lord, still he may not be ranked together with the ancestors.
translations.idiomatic: Superintendent Peng Xuan, Household Supervisor Man Chang, Erudite Zuo Xian, and fifty-three others argued that, below the founding ancestors, five shrines rotate in succession and no later ruler, however worthy, may be classed with those founders.

sentences.zh: 子孫雖欲褒大顯揚而立之，鬼神不饗也。

translations.literal: Though descendants wish to praise, enlarge, display, and raise them, ghosts and spirits will not feast.
translations.idiomatic: Even if descendants try to magnify a later ruler’s cult, the spirits will not accept the sacrifice.

sentences.zh: 孝武皇帝雖有功烈，親盡宜殿。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaowu though he has meritorious blazing deeds, kinship exhausted ought to be removed (from the perpetual shrines).
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu, for all his great deeds, has passed out of the mourning generations and should give way under the rotation of shrines.

sentences.zh: 太僕王舜、中壘校尉劉歆議曰：

translations.literal: Grand coachman Wang Shun and colonel of the central rampart Liu Xin deliberated, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Grand Coachman Wang Shun and Colonel of the Central Rampart Liu Xin offered a counter-memorial:

sentences.zh: 臣聞周室既衰，四夷並侵，獫狁最強，於今匈奴是也。

translations.literal: We have heard that when the Zhou house had declined, the four barbarians together invaded; the Xianyun were strongest—in the present this is the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: We have heard that as Zhou waned, barbarians pressed in from every side; the fiercest were the Xianyun—today’s Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 至宣王而伐之，詩人美而頌之曰「薄伐獫狁，至於太原」，又曰「嘽□推推，如霆如雷，顯允方叔，征伐獫狁，荊蠻來威」，故稱中興。

translations.literal: When it reached King Xuan he campaigned against them; the poets praised and sang, saying, ‘We went to punish the Xianyun, as far as Taiyuan,’ and again said, ‘[Text damaged] rumbling rumbling, like lightning, like thunder—illustrious and true Fang Shu—campaigning against the Xianyun; the Jing Man came in awe’—therefore it is called a mid-dynasty restoration.
translations.idiomatic: King Xuan struck back, and the Odes celebrate him: he drove the Xianyun back to Taiyuan, and Fang Shu’s chariots rolled like thunder until even the southern Man submitted—hence the age is called Zhou’s restoration.

sentences.zh: 及至幽王，犬戎來伐，殺幽王，取宗器。

translations.literal: When it reached King You, the Dog Rong came and attacked, killed King You, took the clan vessels.
translations.idiomatic: Under King You the Dog Rong attacked, slew the king, and carried off the ritual bronzes.

sentences.zh: 自是之後，南夷與北夷交侵，中國不絕如線。

translations.literal: From this after, south barbarians and north barbarians alternately invaded; the central states did not break off like a thread.
translations.idiomatic: Afterward north and south barbarians harried the heartland in turn until China hung by a thread.

sentences.zh: 《春秋》紀齊桓南伐楚，北伐山戎，孔子曰：「微管仲，吾其被髮左衽矣。」

translations.literal: The Spring and Autumn records Duke Huan of Qi campaigning south against Chu, campaigning north against the Mountain Rong; Confucius said, ‘Had it not been for Guan Zhong, we would be letting our hair down and fastening our robes left-over-right.’
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn records how Duke Huan of Qi struck Chu in the south and the Mountain Rong in the north; Confucius said that without Guan Zhong the Chinese would have worn the dress and hair of barbarians.

sentences.zh: 是故棄桓之過而錄其功，以為伯首。

translations.literal: Therefore casting aside Huan’s faults, they recorded his deeds, making him chief among the hegemons.
translations.idiomatic: The classic passes over his faults and records his merit, ranking him first among the hegemons.

sentences.zh: 及漢興，冒頓始強，破東胡，禽月氏，並其土地，地廣兵強，為中國害。

translations.literal: When Han rose, Modu first grew strong, broke the Eastern Hu, captured the Yuezhi, annexed their lands—lands broad, troops strong—becoming a bane to China.
translations.idiomatic: After Han’s founding, Modu built a power that shattered the Eastern Hu, broke the Yuezhi, and swallowed their pasturelands, until the Xiongnu became a broad-realm scourge.

sentences.zh: 南越尉佗總百粵，自稱帝。

translations.literal: The southern Yue commandant Zhao Tuo gathered the hundred Yue and declared himself emperor.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Tuo of Southern Yue united the Yue peoples and took the title of emperor.

sentences.zh: 故中國雖平，猶有四夷之患，且無寧歲。

translations.literal: Therefore though China was pacified, still there was the trouble of the four barbarians, and moreover there were no peaceful years.
translations.idiomatic: Thus even when the interior was quiet, the border peoples kept the realm in arms year after year.

sentences.zh: 一方有急，三面救之，是天下皆動而被其害也。

translations.literal: If one quarter had emergency, three sides rescued it—this is the whole realm all moving and suffering their harm.
translations.idiomatic: When one frontier flared, three others had to march in relief, so the whole empire shook under the strain.

sentences.zh: 孝文皇帝厚以貨賂，與結和親，猶侵暴無已。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaowen richly with goods and bribes, with them knotted peace and kinship—still invasion and violence did not cease.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen sent rich gifts and princess brides, yet the raids never stopped.

sentences.zh: 甚者，興師十餘萬眾，近屯京師及四邊，歲發屯備虜，其為患久矣，非一世之漸也。

translations.literal: In the extreme, raising armies of more than a hundred thousand masses, near encamping at the capital region and the four borders, yearly dispatching garrison to prepare against the barbarians—this as a bane has been long; it is not the gradual growth of one generation.
translations.idiomatic: At worst the court kept hundreds of thousands camped about the capital and the marches, drafting garrison troops every year—a chronic evil, not the work of a single reign.

sentences.zh: 諸侯郡守連匈奴及百粵以為逆者非一人也。

translations.literal: Feudal lords and commandery governors who linked Xiongnu and hundred Yue to make rebellion were not one man.
translations.idiomatic: More than one feudal lord or governor has allied with the Xiongnu or the Yue to rebel.

sentences.zh: 匈奴所殺郡守、都尉，略取人民，不可勝數。

translations.literal: Those whom the Xiongnu killed—commandery governors, commandants—and plundered and took the people cannot be counted.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu have slain governors and commandants and carried off subjects beyond counting.

sentences.zh: 孝武皇帝愍中國罷勞無安寧之時，乃遣大將軍、驃騎、伏波、樓船之屬，南滅百粵，起七郡；

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaowu pitied that China was weary and toiled without a time of peace; then he dispatched the great general, the swift cavalry general, the wave-subduing general, the tower-ship type, south destroying the hundred Yue, raising seven commanderies;
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu, grieving that China knew no rest, sent the Grand General, the Swift Cavalry general, the Wave-Subduing and Tower-Ship commanders south to crush the Yue and carve seven new commanderies;

sentences.zh: 北攘匈奴，降昆邪十萬之眾，置五屬國，起朔方，以奪其肥饒之地；

translations.literal: north repelling the Xiongnu, reducing Kunye’s host of a hundred thousand to submission, establishing five dependent states, raising Shuofang, thereby seizing their fat and fertile lands;
translations.idiomatic: northward he drove the Xiongnu, brought the hundred thousand tribesmen of the Kunye prince to surrender, set up five dependent states, and founded Shuofang to strip them of their richest pastures;

sentences.zh: 東伐朝鮮，起玄菟、樂浪，以斷匈奴之左臂；

translations.literal: east campaigning against Korea, raising Xuantu and Lelang, thereby cutting the Xiongnu’s left arm;
translations.idiomatic: eastward he conquered Korea and founded Xuantu and Lelang to sever the Xiongnu left arm;

sentences.zh: 西伐大宛，並三十六國，結烏孫，起敦煌、酒泉、張掖，以隔婼羌，裂匈奴之右肩。

translations.literal: west campaigning against Dayuan, annexing thirty-six states, knotting Wusun, raising Dunhuang, Jiuquan, Zhangye, thereby blocking the Ruo Qiang, splitting the Xiongnu’s right shoulder.
translations.idiomatic: westward he struck Dayuan, annexed thirty-six states, allied with Wusun, and built Dunhuang, Jiuquan, and Zhangye to wall off the Qiang and break the Xiongnu right wing.

sentences.zh: 單于孤特，遠遁於幕北。

translations.literal: The chanyu was isolated and alone, fled far to north of the desert.
translations.idiomatic: The chanyu stood alone and fled deep beyond the desert.

sentences.zh: 四垂無事，斥地遠境，起十餘郡。

translations.literal: The four margins without affairs; opening land, distant border—raising more than ten commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: The frontiers fell quiet while Han pushed its borders outward and added more than ten new commanderies.

sentences.zh: 功業既定，乃封丞相為富民侯，以大安天下，富實百姓，其規□可見。

translations.literal: Merit and enterprise already fixed—then he enfeoffed the chancellor as marquis of Enriching the People, thereby greatly to pacify the realm, enrich and solidify the hundred surnames; his planning and scope can be seen.
translations.idiomatic: When those deeds were done, he ennobled the chancellor as Marquis of Enriching the People to settle the realm and fill the common people’s granaries—so plain was his design.

sentences.zh: 又招集天下賢俊，與協心同謀，興制度，改正朔，易服色，立天下之祠，建封禪，殊官號，存周後，定諸侯之制，永無逆爭之心，至今累世賴之。

translations.literal: Moreover he summoned and gathered the realm’s worthy and outstanding, with them united heart and same planning, raised institutions, corrected the first month of the year, changed robe and banner colors, established sacrifices under Heaven, erected feng and shan, differentiated office titles, preserved Zhou’s heirs, fixed the feudal lords’ system—forever without rebellious contentious mind—down to the present successive generations rely on it.
translations.idiomatic: He rallied the empire’s best minds, refashioned institutions, reformed the calendar and court dress, founded the grand sacrifices, performed the feng and shan rites on Mount Tai, overhauled titles, preserved the house of Zhou’s descendants, fixed the laws for feudal lords, and left a legacy that still steadies the realm generation after generation.

sentences.zh: 單于守籓，百蠻服從，萬世之基也，中興之功未有高焉者也。

translations.literal: The chanyu guards the fence; the hundred barbarians submit and obey—it is the foundation of myriad generations; of mid-dynasty restoration’s merit none is higher than this.
translations.idiomatic: The chanyu now keeps to his tributary role and the hundred barbarians obey—a foundation meant to last ten thousand generations—and no restoration in history has surpassed this achievement.

sentences.zh: 高帝建大業，為太祖；

translations.literal: Gaodi established the great enterprise—he is Grand Founder;
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu founded the dynasty and stands as Grand Founder;

sentences.zh: 孝文皇帝德至厚也，為文太宗；

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaowen’s virtue reached utmost thickness—he is Wen’s Grand Exemplar;
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen’s virtue ran deepest and he is honored as Grand Exemplar;

sentences.zh: 孝武皇帝功至著也，為武世宗，此孝宣帝所以發德音也。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaowu’s merit reached utmost display—he is Wu’s Shizong; this is why Emperor Xiaoxuan issued fine pronouncements.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu’s achievements were the most conspicuous and he is honored as Shizong—this is why Emperor Xuan proclaimed those august titles.

sentences.zh: 《禮記‧王制》及《春秋穀梁傳》，天子七廟，諸侯五，大夫三，士二。

translations.literal: The Book of Rites, Royal Regulations, and the Spring and Autumn Guliang Commentary: the Son of Heaven seven temples, feudal lords five, grandees three, gentlemen two.
translations.idiomatic: The Royal Regulations chapter of the Book of Rites and the Guliang Commentary prescribe seven shrines for the Son of Heaven, five for feudal lords, three for grandees, and two for gentlemen.

sentences.zh: 天子七日而殯，七月而葬；

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven seven days then encoffining, seven months then burial;
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven lies in state seven days and is buried after seven months;

sentences.zh: 諸侯五日而殯，五月而葬。

translations.literal: feudal lords five days then encoffining, five months then burial.
translations.idiomatic: feudal lords five days in state and five months to the grave.

sentences.zh: 此喪事尊卑之序也，與廟數相應。

translations.literal: This is the sequence of high and low in mourning affairs; it corresponds with the temple counts.
translations.idiomatic: Such is the gradation of mourning by rank, matching the number of shrines each station may keep.

sentences.zh: 其文曰：「天子三昭三穆，與太祖之廟而七；

translations.literal: The text says: The Son of Heaven has three zhao and three mu, together with the Grand Founder’s temple making seven;
translations.idiomatic: The canonical text says the Son of Heaven keeps three zhao and three mu halls plus the Grand Founder, for seven shrines in all;

sentences.zh: 諸侯二昭二穆，與太祖之廟而五。」

translations.literal: feudal lords have two zhao and two mu, together with the Grand Founder’s temple making five.
translations.idiomatic: feudal lords keep two zhao and two mu with the Grand Founder, for five in all.

sentences.zh: 故德厚者流光，德薄者流卑。

translations.literal: Therefore those thick in virtue shed radiance far; those thin in virtue shed low.
translations.idiomatic: Thus deep virtue casts its light far, while shallow virtue reaches only a little way.

sentences.zh: 《春秋左氏傳》曰：「名位不同，禮亦異數。」

translations.literal: The Zuo Commentary on the Spring and Autumn says: ‘Titles and stations differ; ritual likewise differs in measure.’
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Commentary says that rank determines the measure of ritual.

sentences.zh: 自上以下，降殺以兩，禮也。

translations.literal: From above to below, diminishing by steps of two—this is ritual.
translations.idiomatic: From the highest rank downward, each step halves the privilege—such is ritual propriety.

sentences.zh: 七者，其正法數，可常數者也。

translations.literal: Seven is their correct law-number, what can be constantly numbered.
translations.idiomatic: Seven is the canonical count that may stand as a fixed rule.

sentences.zh: 宗不在此數中。

translations.literal: The ‘exemplar’ (zong) is not within this count.
translations.idiomatic: The special cult of an ‘exemplar’ (zong) lies outside that number.

sentences.zh: 宗，變也，苟有功德則宗之，不可預為設數。

translations.literal: Zong is change; if there is merit and virtue then they zong him—one cannot beforehand set a number.
translations.idiomatic: ‘Zong’ is an exceptional honor: whoever has outstanding merit may receive it, and no quota can be fixed in advance.

sentences.zh: 故於殷，太甲為太宗，大戊曰中宗，武丁曰高宗。

translations.literal: Therefore in Yin, Taijia was Grand Exemplar, Da Wu was called Middle Exemplar, Wu Ding was called High Exemplar.
translations.idiomatic: Among the Yin, Taijia was revered as Grand Exemplar, Da Wu as Middle Exemplar, and Wu Ding as High Exemplar.

sentences.zh: 周公為《毋逸》之戒，舉殷三宗以勸成王。

translations.literal: The Duke of Zhou made the admonition of ‘Do Not Slacken,’ raising Yin’s three exemplars to encourage King Cheng.
translations.idiomatic: The Duke of Zhou, in ‘Do Not Slacken,’ cited Yin’s three exemplars to instruct King Cheng.

sentences.zh: 繇是言之，宗無數也，然則所以勸帝者之功德博矣。

translations.literal: From this speaking, zong has no number; then as for that whereby one encourages the emperor’s merit and virtue—it is broad.
translations.idiomatic: Thus the zong title is not capped by number, and the field for rewarding an emperor’s merit is wide indeed.

sentences.zh: 以七廟言之，孝武皇帝未宜殿；

translations.literal: Speaking by the seven temples, Emperor Xiaowu is not yet fit to be removed;
translations.idiomatic: Judged by the rule of seven shrines, Emperor Wu’s tablet should not yet be rotated out;

sentences.zh: 以所宗言之，則不可謂無功德。

translations.literal: speaking by what is zonged, then one cannot say there is no merit and virtue.
translations.idiomatic: judged by the standard for a zong cult, no one can call him lacking in merit.

sentences.zh: 《禮記》祀典曰：「夫聖王之制祀也，功施於民則祀之，以勞定國則祀之，能救大災則祀之。」

translations.literal: The Book of Rites, Canon of Sacrifice says: ‘As for the sage king’s instituting sacrifice: if merit extends to the people then sacrifice him; if by toil he settled the state then sacrifice him; if able to rescue from great calamity then sacrifice him.’
translations.idiomatic: The Canon of Sacrifice in the Book of Rites says that sage kings enshrine those whose bounty reached the people, who ordered the state by their labor, or who saved the realm from great disaster.

sentences.zh: 竊觀孝武皇帝，功德皆兼而有焉。

translations.literal: We humbly observe Emperor Xiaowu—merit and virtue he both combined and possessed therein.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu meets every one of those tests of merit and virtue.

sentences.zh: 凡在於異姓，猶將特祀之，況於先祖？

translations.literal: Whenever it lies with a different surname, still they will specially sacrifice him—how much more with an ancestor?
translations.idiomatic: Men of other surnames would still merit a special cult—how much more a dynastic forebear?

sentences.zh: 或說天子五廟無見文，又說中宗、高宗者，宗其道而毀其廟。

translations.literal: Some say the Son of Heaven’s five temples have no visible text; again some say of Middle Exemplar and High Exemplar—they zong their Way but destroy their temples.
translations.idiomatic: Some argue that a five-shrine rule for the Son of Heaven lacks clear textual support, and that Middle and High Exemplars of Yin were honored in name while their shrines were still abolished.

sentences.zh: 名與實異，非尊德貴功之意也。

translations.literal: Name and reality differ—this is not the intent of honoring virtue and prizing merit.
translations.idiomatic: That would split title from substance and defeat the very purpose of honoring virtue and achievement.

sentences.zh: 《詩》云：「蔽芾甘棠，勿剪勿伐，邵伯所茇。」

translations.literal: The Odes says: ‘Little and lush is the sweet pear—do not trim, do not hew—there the Earl of Shao rested.’
translations.idiomatic: The Odes says of the pear tree beneath which the Earl of Shao once rested: do not lop or cut its boughs.

sentences.zh: 思其人猶愛其樹，況宗其道而毀其廟乎？

translations.literal: Thinking of the man, still they love his tree—how much more zonging his Way and destroying his temple?
translations.idiomatic: Later generations loved the tree for love of the man; how then can we honor his Way and tear down his shrine?

sentences.zh: 迭毀之禮自有常法，無殊功異德，固以親疏相推及。

translations.literal: The ritual of rotating destruction itself has constant law; without extraordinary merit or unusual virtue, solidly by close and distant kin it pushes and reaches.
translations.idiomatic: The rotation of shrines follows a fixed rule for rulers of ordinary merit, advancing by degrees of kinship alone.

sentences.zh: 至祖宗之序，多少之數，經傳無明文，至尊至重，難以疑文虛說定也。

translations.literal: As for the ordering of ancestors and forebears, the numbers of more and less—the classics and commentaries have no clear text; utmost venerable, utmost weighty—hard to settle by doubtful texts and empty theories.
translations.idiomatic: The classics give no explicit count for ordering founder shrines; the matter is too grave to settle on doubtful glosses.

sentences.zh: 孝宣皇帝舉公卿之議，用眾儒之謀，既以為世宗之廟，建之萬世，宣佈天下。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaoxuan raised the deliberation of dukes and ministers, employed the planning of the host of scholars; already he made it the Shizong temple, established it for myriad generations, proclaimed it within the four seas.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan took counsel from his high ministers and the Confucian academicians, named the shrine Shizong, and proclaimed to the empire that it should stand forever.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為孝武皇帝功烈如彼，孝宣皇帝崇立之如此，不宜毀。

translations.literal: We your servants foolishly consider that Emperor Xiaowu’s meritorious blazing deeds were such as those, Emperor Xiaoxuan’s elevating and establishing him was such as this—it is not fitting to destroy.
translations.idiomatic: We hold that Emperor Wu’s achievements were of that order, Emperor Xuan’s elevation of him so explicit, and the shrine ought not to be abolished.

sentences.zh: 上覽其議而從之。

translations.literal: The sovereign perused their deliberation and followed it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor read their memorial and agreed.

sentences.zh: 制曰：「太僕舜、中壘校尉歆議可。」

translations.literal: The edict said: “Grand Coachman Shun and Colonel of the Central Rampart Xin’s deliberation is approved.
translations.idiomatic: An edict ruled: “The proposal of Grand Coachman Wang Shun and Colonel Liu Xin is accepted.

sentences.zh: 歆又以為「禮，去事有殺，故《春秋外傳》曰：『日祭，月祀，時享，歲貢，終王』祖檷則日祭，曾高則月祀，二祧則時享，壇墠則歲貢，大禘則終王。

translations.literal: Xin further considered that by ritual, as one leaves behind events, observances are reduced; therefore the Outer Commentary to the Spring and Autumn says: daily sacrifice, monthly offering, seasonal feast, yearly tribute, and the final great offering at a king’s end—grandfather and father receive daily sacrifice, great-great and high ancestors monthly offering, the two tiao seasonal feasting, mound and field altars yearly tribute, and the great di at the end of a reign.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xin added that ritual thins with distance, citing the Guoyu formula of daily, monthly, seasonal, and yearly rites capped by the great di, each grade of ancestry matched to its proper frequency.

sentences.zh: 德盛而游廣，親親之殺也；

translations.literal: Virtue abundant and roaming broad—this is the reduction of loving kin;
translations.idiomatic: Where virtue is great, the circuit of sacrifice widens, and love of kin is graded by nearness;

sentences.zh: 彌遠則彌尊，故禘為重矣。

translations.literal: the farther, the more venerated—therefore the di is weightiest.
translations.idiomatic: the more remote the ancestor, the more solemn the rite, so the great di stands highest of all.

sentences.zh: 孫居王父之處，正昭穆，則孫常與祖相代，此遷廟之殺也。

translations.literal: The grandson occupies the king-father’s place; correcting zhao-mu, then the grandson constantly alternates with the grandfather—this is the reduction of relocating temples.
translations.idiomatic: When a grandson steps into his grandfather’s zhao-mu place, the two generations trade rank in the rotation—such is the logic by which shrines are moved.

sentences.zh: 聖人於其祖，出於情矣，禮無所不順，故無毀廟。

translations.literal: The sage toward his ancestor—emerging from feeling; ritual has nothing it does not follow—therefore there are no destroyed temples.
translations.idiomatic: The sage’s feeling for his forebears is complete, and ritual leaves no proper sentiment unfollowed, so no true shrine of the heart is ever simply destroyed.

sentences.zh: 自貢禹建迭毀之議，惠、景及太上寢園廢而為虛，失禮意矣。」

translations.literal: From when Gong Yu established the rotating-destruction proposal, Hui, Jing, and the Grand Supreme resting-parks were abolished and became empty—this lost ritual’s intent.
translations.idiomatic: Ever since Gong Yu’s policy of rotating abolitions, the mausoleum parks of Emperors Hui and Jing and the Grand Supreme Emperor have lain abandoned—utterly at odds with the spirit of the rites.

sentences.zh: 至平帝元始中，大司馬王莽奏：「本始元年丞相義等議，謚孝宣皇帝親曰悼園，置邑三百家，至元康元年，丞相相等奏，父為士，子為天子，祭以天子，悼園宜稱尊號曰『皇考』，立廟，益故奉園民滿千六百家，以為縣。

translations.literal: When it reached Emperor Ping’s Yuanshi era, grand marshal Wang Mang submitted: “In the first year of Benshi Chancellor Yi and others deliberated, posthumously naming Emperor Xuan’s father the Dao Park, establishing a settlement of three hundred households. In the first year of Yuankang Chancellor Xiang and others submitted: the father being a shi, the son being Son of Heaven—sacrifice with Son of Heaven rites—the Dao Park ought to receive the exalted title ‘Imperial Father,’ establish a temple, increase the former park-serving people to a full sixteen hundred households, making it a county.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Ping, Wang Mang memorialized that in 73 BCE Chancellor Cai Yi had posthumously titled Emperor Xuan’s father the Dao domain with three hundred households, and that in 65 BCE Chancellor Wei Xiang had argued that a father who died a commoner but whose son became emperor should receive imperial rites—the domain should be titled Imperial Father, given its own shrine, and its caretakers raised to sixteen hundred households as a county seat.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為皇考廟本不當立，累世奉之，非是。

translations.literal: We your servants foolishly consider that the Imperial Father temple originally ought not to have been established; successive generations serving it—is not correct.
translations.idiomatic: We hold that the shrine to the Imperial Father should never have been founded; to maintain it for generation after generation was wrong.

sentences.zh: 又孝文太后南陵、孝昭太后雲陵園，雖前以禮不復修，陵名未正。

translations.literal: Moreover Empress Dowager Xiaowen’s Nanling and Empress Dowager Xiaozhao’s Yunling mausoleum parks—though formerly by ritual they were not again maintained, the tomb names were not corrected.
translations.idiomatic: The mausoleum parks of Empress Dowager Bo at Nanling and of Empress Dowager Zhao at Yunling, though rites had already allowed them to lapse, still bear irregular titles.

sentences.zh: 謹與大司徒晏等百四十七人議，皆曰孝宣皇帝以兄孫繼統為孝昭皇帝後，以數，故孝元世以孝景皇帝及皇考廟親未盡，不毀。

translations.literal: Respectfully with grand minister of education Yan and one hundred forty-seven others deliberated, all saying Emperor Xuan, as elder brother’s grandson, continued the line as heir after Emperor Zhao; by the reckoning, therefore in Emperor Yuan’s generation Emperor Jing’s temple and the Imperial Father temple—kin not yet exhausted—were not destroyed.
translations.idiomatic: We have consulted Grand Minister of Education Yan and 147 others, who note that Emperor Xuan entered the succession as Emperor Zhao’s heir from another branch; by generational count, Emperor Yuan therefore left Emperor Jing’s shrine and the Imperial Father shrine standing because mourning ties had not yet run out.

sentences.zh: 此兩統貳父，違於禮制。

translations.literal: This is two lineages and a doubled father—violating ritual regulation.
translations.idiomatic: That created two legitimate lines and two fathers for one heir, which classical ritual forbids.

sentences.zh: 案義奏親謚曰『悼』，裁置奉邑，皆應經義。

translations.literal: Examining Yi’s memorial, the close kin’s posthumous title was called ‘Dao,’ cutting and setting the serving settlement—all responded to classical meaning.
translations.idiomatic: Cai Yi’s memorial had posthumously titled the prince ‘Dao,’ limited the tomb domain to a modest settlement, and matched what the classics allow.

sentences.zh: 相奏悼園稱『皇考』，立廟，益民為縣，違離祖統，乖繆本義。

translations.literal: Xiang’s memorial called the Dao Park ‘Imperial Father,’ established a temple, increased people to make a county—violating and departing from the ancestral lineage, deviating wrong from the root meaning.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiang’s later memorial elevated the Dao domain to ‘Imperial Father,’ founded a separate shrine, and swelled its staff into a county—severing the line from the true imperial ancestry and twisting the original intent of the rule.

sentences.zh: 父為士，子為天子，祭以天子者，乃謂若虞舜、夏禹、殷湯、周文、漢之高祖受命而王者也，非謂繼祖統為後者也。

translations.literal: ‘Father being a shi, son being Son of Heaven, sacrificing with Son of Heaven rites’—this speaks of such as Shun of Yu, Yu of Xia, Tang of Yin, Wen of Zhou, Han’s Gaodi—those who received the mandate and became king; it does not speak of continuing the ancestor’s lineage as heir.
translations.idiomatic: The formula ‘a commoner father and an emperor son, honored with imperial sacrifice’ applies to founders such as Shun, Yu, Tang, King Wen, and Han Gaozu, who received the mandate as kings; it was never meant for an heir who merely continues another emperor’s line.

sentences.zh: 臣請皇高祖考廟奉明園毀勿修，罷南陵、雲陵為縣。」

translations.literal: We your servants beg that the Imperial Father’s temple in the Fengming mausoleum park be destroyed and not maintained, and that Nanling and Yunling be reduced to ordinary counties.
translations.idiomatic: We ask that the shrine to Emperor Xuan’s father in the Fengming tomb park be torn down and not rebuilt, and that the Nanling and Yunling mausoleum districts be turned into regular counties.

sentences.zh: 奏可。

translations.literal: The memorial was approved.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor approved the memorial.

sentences.zh: 司徒掾班彪曰：漢承亡秦絕學之後，祖宗之制因時施宜。

translations.literal: Recorder to the minister of education Ban Biao said: Han received after perished Qin and cut-off learning; the institutions of the ancestors accorded with the times and applied what was suitable.
translations.idiomatic: Ban Biao, a clerk in the ministry of education, observed that Han inherited a realm in which Qin had nearly extinguished learning, so its ancestral institutions had to be adapted to circumstance.

sentences.zh: 自元、成後學者蕃滋，貢禹毀宗廟，匡衡改郊兆，何武定三公，後皆數復，故紛紛不定。

translations.literal: From Yuan and Cheng afterward scholars multiplied; Gong Yu destroyed ancestral temples, Kuang Heng changed suburban altar sites, He Wu fixed the three dukes—afterward all repeatedly restored, therefore tangled and unsettled.
translations.idiomatic: After Emperors Yuan and Cheng, scholarship flourished: Gong Yu pared the shrines, Kuang Heng shifted the suburban altars, He Wu fixed the three dukes—yet each reform was undone and redone, so policy seesawed without rest.

sentences.zh: 何者？

translations.literal: What was the reason?
translations.idiomatic: Why was that?

sentences.zh: 禮文缺微，古今異制，各為一家，未易可偏定也。

translations.literal: Ritual texts are deficient and subtle; ancient and modern differ in institutions; each becomes one school—not easy to fix by partial bias.
translations.idiomatic: The ritual canon is laconic and ambiguous, ancient usage differs from Han practice, and every school claims the truth, so no partisan verdict can easily settle the matter.

sentences.zh: 考觀諸儒之議，劉歆博而篤矣。

translations.literal: Examining and viewing the various scholars’ deliberations, Liu Xin was broad and solid.
translations.idiomatic: Reviewing the scholars’ debates, Liu Xin’s learning was the widest and his conclusions the soundest.
